title: Processes of change in cognitive-behavioral psychotherapy for procrastination: moderation and longitudinal mediation analyses of randomized controlled trial data
abstract:
Objective. Procrastination is a common self-regulatory difficulty leading to impaired well-being. Although cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is considered the most promising intervention for procrastination, it remains unclear whether outcomes vary across individuals and which mechanisms drive therapeutic change. To address these gaps, we synthesized key mechanistic accounts of procrastination in an integrative cognitive-behavioral (ICB-P) model outlining processes involved in development and persistence of procrastination. We then comprehensively examined the processes of change in CBT for procrastination.
Methods. Candidate moderators and mechanisms of therapeutic change were examined in secondary analyses of pooled data (N = 459) from two randomized controlled trials comparing CBT for academic procrastination with a wait-list control. Procrastination severity and a wide set of candidate mechanisms were assessed at pre-, mid-, and post-intervention. Moderation analyses and longitudinal structural equation models with contemporaneous (within-wave) and lagged (cross-wave) mediation paths were applied.
Results. Participants with greater baseline emotion regulation difficulties benefited more from the intervention, whereas dysfunctional beliefs associated with some cluster B personality disorders were linked to lower improvements. Baseline depression, anxiety, or ADHD symptoms did not moderate outcomes. Regarding mechanisms of change, increased task value and proactive control mediated both contemporaneous and lagged reductions in procrastination. Contemporaneous effects were mediated through increased positive and decreased negative task-related affect, improved perseverance, emotional clarity, and internal attributions of success.
Conclusions. Findings suggest broad applicability of CBT for procrastination. Optimized interventions could enhance the implicated mechanisms, i.e. target task-related affect and self-regulation, or incorporate additional components. Directions for future research are discussed.

        ABSTRACT


       Objective. Procrastination is a common self-regulatory difficulty leading to impaired
well-being. Although cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) is considered the most promising
intervention for procrastination, it remains unclear whether outcomes vary across individuals
and which mechanisms drive therapeutic change. To address these gaps, we synthesized key
mechanistic accounts of procrastination in an integrative cognitive-behavioral (ICB-P) model

comprehensively examined the processes of change in CBT for procrastination.

in secondary analyses of pooled data (N = 459) from two randomized controlled trials
comparing CBT for academic procrastination with a wait-list control. Procrastination severity
and a wide set of candidate mechanisms were assessed at pre-, mid-, and post-intervention.
Moderation analyses and longitudinal structural equation models with contemporaneous
(within-wave) and lagged (cross-wave) mediation paths were applied.
       Results. Participants with greater baseline emotion regulation difficulties benefited
more from the intervention, whereas dysfunctional beliefs associated with some cluster B

attributions of success.
       Conclusions. Findings suggest broad applicability of CBT for procrastination.
Optimized interventions could enhance the implicated mechanisms, i.e. target task-related
affect and self-regulation, or incorporate additional components. Directions for future research
are discussed.




       KEYWORDS


cognitive-behavioral therapy, procrastination, mechanisms of change, treatment moderators,
treatment mediators, structural equation models, randomized controlled trial



                                                                                              2
    HIGHLIGHTS


● The proposed ICB-P model offers an extensive mechanistic account of procrastination.
● CBT effects were stronger for individuals with more emotion regulation difficulties.
● Affective or ADHD symptoms did not moderate outcomes, suggesting broad
   applicability.
● Changes in task-related affect emerged as key pathways of improvement.
● Findings can guide targeted and effective interventions for procrastination.




                                                                                         3
        INTRODUCTION


       Procrastination is most often defined as the voluntary yet irrational delay of starting or
completing an intended task despite anticipating negative consequences (Klingsieck, 2013;
Mahy et al., 2024). For an estimated 15–20% of the population (Ferrari et al., 2005)
procrastination becomes a persistent problem linked to significant impairments in quality of
life. It is especially prevalent among students: about 75% report procrastinating regularly
(American College Health Association, 2022), and 10% were identified as pathological
procrastinators (see Rist et al., 2023). This subgroup reported frequent delays of important
tasks, excessive time spent procrastinating, reliance on time pressure, more procrastination-
related physical and psychological complaints, below-potential performance, and significant
interference with personal goals. Overall, negative effects of procrastination were found to
manifest in lower academic and occupational performance, financial losses, poorer physical
health, difficulties in personal relationships, and elevated symptoms of depression, anxiety,
stress, as well as poor sleep, lower self-esteem, and reduced satisfaction with personal
achievements (see Mahy et al., 2024 for review).
       As many individuals struggle to manage procrastination and its psychological
consequences, they often seek appropriate psychotherapeutic support. In addition to individual
help-seeking, given the prevalence and widespread consequences of academic procrastination,
academic institutions are increasingly interested in cost-effective treatments and prevention
programs (Turner & Hodis, 2023; Zacks & Hen, 2018). As a result, a variety of individual,
group and internet-based interventions have been proposed, with heterogeneous results
regarding their efficacy (for meta-analysis, see Rozental et al., 2018; van Eerde & Klingsieck,
2018; Malouff & Schutte, 2019; or for systematic review see Turner & Hodis, 2023). Among
these, cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) have shown more homogenous medium-to-large
effects compared with wait-list controls. Accordingly, meta-analyses identify CBT as one of
the most promising approaches for treating procrastination (Rozental et al. 2018); van Eerde
and Klingsieck, 2018).
       At the same time, little is known about whether – and to what extent – those CBT
techniques adequately target the mechanisms that underlie and maintain problematic
procrastination. Similarly, moderation evidence on whether treatment outcomes differ across
subgroups is limited. Meanwhile, clarifying these issues is crucial for optimizing and tailoring
treatment approaches, for instance by emphasizing the most effective components, removing
less impactful ones, or selecting treatment strategies that target key mechanisms relevant to

                                                                                               4
 individual patients’ symptoms (Kazdin, 2007; Windgassen et al., 2016; Solomonov, 2025).
Such improvements are particularly important given the substantial number of non-responders
to existing treatments not only for procrastination (Browarczyk et al., 2026) but also across
other disorders (see e.g. Cuijpers et al., 2021; 2024 for meta-analyses). Thus, clinical trials
should not only rigorously assess the efficacy of intervention protocols, but also explore and
empirically validate the mechanisms through which interventions address symptomatology and
produce therapeutic change.
       The goal of the current paper is to advance understanding of outcome moderators and
candidate mechanisms of change in CBT interventions for procrastination. First, we review key
mechanistic models of procrastination and their implications for intervention. We then
introduce an integrative cognitive-behavioral model that synthesizes these perspectives. Next,
we review the current empirical evidence on moderators and mechanisms of change in
procrastination treatment. Finally, we extend this evidence by examining a broad scope of both
candidate moderators and mechanisms of change from two consecutive randomized controlled
trials (RCTs) of a 5-session group CBT intervention, using a large pooled sample (N = 459).
Together, the present work offers the most comprehensive empirical examination to date of
moderators and mechanisms of change in CBT for procrastination.


       Theoretical models of procrastination and candidate mechanisms of its treatment


       Although the emerging theoretical frameworks have substantially deepened our
understanding of procrastination in recent decades, the extent to which they inform
psychotherapeutic interventions varies. Van Eerde and Klingsieck (2018) note that only some
of the interventions studied so far (e.g. CBT-based) were conceptually derived from
mechanistic models of procrastination, while others appeared to target only its selected
correlates. Another current approach to treating procrastination is to use more general protocols
corresponding to a given therapeutic modality, such as Acceptance and Commitment Therapy
(ACT), that do not aim to address procrastination-specific mechanisms but instead target
transdiagnostic mechanisms such as psychological flexibility (e.g. Asikainen et al., 2024).
Given the availability of detailed theoretical models, however, there is increasing opportunity
to design interventions that more precisely target mechanisms specific to procrastination and
potentially improve treatment outcomes.
       Foremost, procrastination is a form of self-regulatory failure – a dysfunction in
cognitive, emotional, and behavioral processes that help optimize mental and physiological

                                                                                               5
 functioning – which results in undesired delays in achieving set goals. As Mahy et al. (2024)
suggest, the prevailing models offer two complementary perspectives on procrastination.
       The first perspective comes from general decision-making models, which provide
insight into why people may choose to act on or delay their goals. A characteristic of these
models is that they do not explicitly describe procrastination as maladaptive, as they often do
not consider its negative consequences or habitual nature. These models include the widely
recognized Temporal Motivation Theory (TMT; Steel & König, 2006; Steel, 2007) and its later
extensions, such as the Temporal Decision Model (TDM; Zhang et al., 2019). TMT proposes
that the motivation to perform a task at a given time (task utility) increases with the perceived
personal worth of the task (task value) and the confidence that one’s action will lead to the
desired outcome (success expectancy; also understood as self-efficacy). However, motivation
decreases with greater temporal gap between now and the moment when the action will be
rewarded or punished (delay) multiplied by the individual tendency to prefer immediate
gratification (delay sensitivity; understood also as an aspect of impulsivity). TDM (Zhang et al.,
2019) adds the perceived task aversiveness, arguing that due to the discounting of negative
attitudes toward future tasks, people are likely to postpone them as they imagine them to be less
aversive in the future than right now.
       Although these models were not directly developed to guide interventions, they can
provide several treatment implications (Rozental & Carlbring, 2014; Rozental et al., 2015;
Rozental et al., 2018, de Haas et al., 2025). Increasing self-efficacy and perceived task value,
as well as strengthening tolerance for delayed gratification (and, in TDM, improving
management of negative task-related affect), could foster the motivation needed for undertaking
a task when time is still available. For example, positive experiences with gradual
implementation of goal-directed behaviors into the daily schedule could build self-efficacy;
setting personally meaningful long-term goals and breaking them into manageable subgoals
could reduce task aversiveness and strengthen the sense of value of completing work; limiting
distractions (e.g. cues for instantly rewarding activities) and implementing reward systems
could help overcome sensitivity to delayed gratification. Because these theories describe
procrastination as an active choice between competing activities, interventions may also benefit
from reducing decision points, e.g. through scheduling work to be done in a specific time and
location (Rozental & Carlbring, 2014; Rozental et al., 2018). As argued by Rozental et al.
(2018), CBT-based approaches naturally incorporate these strategies and are therefore well-
positioned to target mechanisms outlined in TMT and TDM, making them particularly suitable
for treating procrastination.

                                                                                                6
        The second category of models focuses on the emotional and motivational processes
that cause and sustain procrastination. These models aim to explain why procrastination can
become recurring and difficult to overcome. A central example is the short-term mood repair
theory (Sirois and Pychyl, 2013), which proposes that when negative emotions arise at the
prospect of performing an aversive task, one possible regulation strategy is to engage in another
valuable or more pleasurable alternative activity, or to simply avoid the thought of performing
the task. Although this strategy allows for short-term relief, in the long term it proves to be
maladaptive, as procrastination leads to distress that tends to outweigh the temporary gains from
mood improvement (Tice & Baumeister, 1997). Thus, procrastination reflects prioritizing
present emotional relief over future well-being. Based on this theory, emotion-regulation skills
training has been proposed as a method for overcoming procrastination through managing
negative emotions and discomfort without disengaging from long-term goals (Eckert et al.,
2016; Schuenemann et al., 2022).
       Finally, cognitive-behavioral models of procrastination, such as the framework
proposed by Höcker et al. (2017; 2022), build on the mood-regulation premise (Sirois & Pychyl,
2013) but conceptualize procrastination more specifically as a learned maladaptive coping
behavior (Svartdal et al., 2018). In this paper, we present an integrative cognitive-behavioral
model of procrastination (ICB-P model) that extends the original Höcker et al. (2017; 2022)
model by incorporating the decision-making paths suggested in TMT, as well as short- and
long-term processes that reinforce either behavioral delay or goal-directed behavior.
       In the ICB-P model (see Figure 1), individual vulnerability factors, past experiences,
and situational contexts shape the cognitive, emotional and physiological responses to task-
related stimuli. These responses then impact the perceived time availability, task value, task
aversiveness, and success expectancy – factors that determine task motivation in TMT and
TDM. When

title: Psychosocial Factors Associated With Camouflaging and Its Relationship With Mental Health and Well-Being in Autistic and Non-autistic People: A Mixed Methods Systematic Review
abstract: Camouflaging involves hiding one’s autistic characteristics in social situations and is associated with poorer mental health in autistic people. Various psychosocial factors are likely implicated in camouflaging and its impact on well-being. This mixed methods systematic review aimed to synthesize qualitative and quantitative research on psychosocial factors associated with camouflaging and its relationship with mental well-being in autistic and non-autistic people. Six electronic databases were searched. Following a convergent integrated approach, quantitative data were transformed and synthesized with qualitative data to perform thematic synthesis. Fifty-eight studies (40 qualitative, 13 quantitative, and five mixed methods) were included. We identified three themes on psychosocial correlates of camouflaging: (1) social norms and pressures of a largely non-autistic world, (2) social acceptance and rejection, and (3) self-esteem and identity; and four themes on psychosocial consequences of camouflaging for mental well-being: (1) a pragmatic way of exerting individual agency and control; (2) overlooked, under-supported, and burnt out; (3) impact on social relationships; and (4) low self-esteem and identity confusion. Camouflaging is a largely socially driven response associated with negative psychological and social consequences. A whole society approach is needed to promote a culture of acceptance and support autistic individuals to express their authentically autistic selves.

                                            Highlights


   •   Autistic people camouflage due to social pressures, stigma, and the need to belong.

   •   Some view camouflaging as a pragmatic way to exert individual agency and control.

   •   But camouflaging leads to burnout and adversely affects relationships and identity.

   •   Societies can reduce pressures to camouflage through autism acceptance.
 PSYCHOSOCIAL INFLUENCES ON CAMOUFLAGING                                                                   4


                                               Abstract

Camouflaging involves hiding one’s autistic characteristics in social situations and is associated with

poorer mental health in autistic people. Various psychosocial factors are likely implicated in

camouflaging and its impact on well-being. This mixed methods systematic review aimed to

synthesize qualitative and quantitative research on psychosocial factors associated with

camouflaging and its relationship with mental well-being in autistic and non-autistic people. Six

electronic databases were searched. Following a convergent integrated approach, quantitative data

were transformed and synthesized with qualitative data to perform thematic synthesis. Fifty-eight

studies (40 qualitative, 13 quantitative, and five mixed methods) were included. We identified three

themes on psychosocial correlates of camouflaging: (1) social norms and pressures of a largely non-

autistic world, (2) social acceptance and rejection, and (3) self-esteem and identity; and four themes

on psychosocial consequences of camouflaging for mental well-being: (1) a pragmatic way of

exerting individual agency and control; (2) overlooked, under-supported, and burnt out; (3) impact

on social relationships; and (4) low self-esteem and identity confusion. Camouflaging is a largely

socially driven response associated with negative psychological and social consequences. A whole

society approach is needed to promote a culture of acceptance and support autistic individuals to

express their authentically autistic selves.

        Keywords: autism, camouflaging, masking, psychosocial, mental health, well-being
 PSYCHOSOCIAL INFLUENCES ON CAMOUFLAGING                                                                        5


    Psychosocial Factors Associated With Camouflaging and Its Relationship With Mental Health and

         Well-Being in Autistic and Non-autistic People: A Mixed Methods Systematic Review

          Autism1 is a complex lifelong neurodevelopmental condition characterized by differences,

strengths, and/or difficulties in social communication and interaction, and highly focused interests

and repetitive behaviors (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). Across their lifespan, autistic

individuals2 may experience challenges in adaptive functioning, language and communication, and

social integration and independence (Howlin & Magiati, 2017; Howlin & Moss, 2012; Magiati et al.,

2014). Compared to the non-autistic population, autistic individuals are at increased risk of mental

health problems, including anxiety and depression (Croen et al., 2015; for review, see Hossain et al.,

2020). Drawing on the social model of disability (Oliver, 1990), autistic individuals face poorer

psychosocial outcomes not only or primarily because of their autistic differences but also

substantially due to the high levels of stigma and discrimination they often experience.

          Autistic people are significantly more likely to experience vulnerability to various negative

life experiences, such as bullying victimization, social rejection, and isolation (Griffiths et al., 2019;

Maïano et al., 2016; Orsmond et al., 2013). To cope with these negative social experiences, some

autistic people have described developing strategies to “mask” or “camouflage” their autism and

“pass as normal” in a predominantly non-autistic world (Cage & Troxell-Whitman, 2019; Hull et al.,

2017). Camouflaging3 refers to the conscious or unconscious use of strategies by autistic people to

minimize the visibility of their autistic traits in social situations (Hull et al., 2017; Lai et al., 2017).

These strategies include masking one’s autistic behaviors, compensating for autistic social




1
  We use the term ‘autism’ to refer to the clinical diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) as this is the
autistic community’s preference, and because we view autism as a difference and within a neurodiversity lens
(Kenny et al., 2016).
2
  We use identity-first language (e.g., autistic person) to avoid potentially ableist language (Bottema-Beutel et
al., 2021) and to align with the preferences of most autistic adults based on a survey of the U.K. autism
community (Kenny et al., 2016). Nonetheless, we acknowledge that personal language preferences vary, and
there are others who prefer the use of person-first language (e.g., person with autism).
3
  We use “camouflaging” as an umbrella term to describe a set of behaviors that have also been referred to as
“masking” (Pearson & Rose, 2021), “compensation” (Livingston & Happé, 2017), and “adaptive morphing”
(Lawson, 2020) in the literature.
 PSYCHOSOCIAL INFLUENCES ON CAMOUFLAGING                                                                 6


differences (e.g., forcing oneself to make eye contact), and finding ways to blend in and assimilate

with non-autistic people (Hull et al., 2017, 2019). Autistic individuals report camouflaging in both

formal (e.g., at work or school) and informal/interpersonal (e.g., with friends and family) contexts to

increase their social connections, gain acceptance, or avoid ostracism and discrimination (Cage &

Troxell-Whitman, 2019; Hull et al., 2017). The extent to which one engages in camouflaging varies

based on context, with autistic individuals reporting that they generally camouflage less frequently

and extensively once they have established a comfortable relationship with others (Hull et al., 2017).

        A new but rapidly growing area of autism research, camouflaging was initially proposed to

be a plausible reason explaining the under-identification of autism in females (Attwood, 2007; Gould

& Ashton-Smith, 2011), and more recently has been consistently associated with poorer mental well-

being in autistic people (see review by Cook, Hull, et al., 2021). There are currently two main

methodological approaches to measuring camouflaging: (1) internal-external discrepancy methods,

which operationalize camouflaging as the discrepancy between internal (“true”) autistic status (e.g.,

self-reported autistic traits or performance-based social cognitive abilities) and observer-rated social

behavior (e.g., observer ratings on the Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule; Lai et al., 2017); and

(2) self-report measures asking participants the extent to which they engage in camouflaging. The

most used self-report measure in studies to date is the Camouflaging Autistic Traits Questionnaire

(CAT-Q; Hull et al., 2019), which has demonstrated good psychometric properties and measurement

invariance. Several studies have examined self-reported camouflaging in autistic and non-autistic

participants, showing that non-autistic people also engage in camouflaging, albeit to a lesser extent

than autistic people generally (Belcher et al., 2022; Hull et al., 2019; Livingston et al., 2020).

Additionally, regardless of autism diagnosis, increased camouflaging has been found to be associated

with higher levels of autistic traits (Hull et al., 2019; Livingston et al., 2020).

Impact and Consequences of Camouflaging

        Qualitative evidence suggests that camouflaging could be an adaptive social strategy for

some autistic people to manage others’ impressions, fit in with others, and form relationships (Cage
 PSYCHOSOCIAL INFLUENCES ON CAMOUFLAGING                                                                            7


& Troxell-Whitman, 2019; Cook, Crane, et al., 2021; Hull et al., 2017). In addition, for autistic

individuals who find socializing stressful, camouflaging may provide a way for them to get through

social situations “as painlessly and as quickly as possible” (Cage & Troxell-Whitman, 2019, p. 1906).

Autistic people have also reported that camouflaging enables them to achieve life goals, become

more independent, and contribute to society (Hull et al., 2017; Livingston, Shah, & Happé, 2019b).

         However, camouflaging and its associated benefits often come at a serious cost to autistic

people’s mental well-being. Autistic individuals in many qualitative studies have described

camouflaging as draining, often resulting in exhaustion and autistic burnout4 (e.g., Higgins et al.,

2021; Hull et al., 2017). In several quantitative cross-sectional studies, greater self-reported

camouflaging has been consistently associated with poorer mental health—including anxiety,

depression, stress, and suicidality—in autistic and non-autistic adults (Beck et al., 2020; Cassidy et

al., 2018; McQuaid et al., 2022). Similarly, Cage and Troxell-Whitman (2019) found that autistic

adults who reported camouflaging more frequently across both formal and informal contexts

experienced significantly greater anxiety and stress than those who camouflaged less in both

contexts. Utilizing internal-external discrepancy approaches, studies have found that higher

camouflaging was associated with greater depressive symptoms in autistic men but not women (Lai

et al., 2017) and more anxiety symptoms in autistic adolescents (Livingston, Colvert, et al., 2019).

Correlates of Camouflaging

         Guided by the biopsychosocial model (Engel, 1977), it is likely that a combination of

neurobiologically determined and psychosocial factors influences the development and maintenance

of camouflaging and may explain its impact on mental health and well-being.

Individual or Neurobiologically-Based Correlates of Camouflaging

         A small pool of studies has empirically examined individual differences in camouflaging.

Most report no significant correlation between age or age of autism diagnosis and camouflaging


4
  Autistic burnout is a highly debilitating state/condition characterized by chronic exhaustion, loss of skills,
intensifying of one’s autistic traits, reduced tolerance to stimuli, and overall reduced functioning (Higgins et al.,
2021; Mantzalas et al., 2022; Raymaker et al., 2020).
 PSYCHOSOCIAL INFLUENCES ON CAMOUFLAGING                                                                      8


(Hull et al., 2021; Lai et al., 2017; Livingston et al., 2020). Studies investigating sex/gender

differences tend to find somewhat higher levels of camouflaging in autistic females than in males

(Hull et al., 2020; Lai et al., 2017; Schuck et al., 2019). Having an autism diagnosis or greater autistic

traits is consistently associated with more self-reported camouflaging behaviors in autistic and non-

autistic adults (Belcher et al., 2022; Hull et al., 2019; Livingston et al., 2020). Evidence on cognitive

correlates of camouflaging tends to be mixed. Using internal-external discrepancy methods, Lai et al.

(2017) found that greater camouflaging may be related to better executive functioning abilities in

autistic females but not males. In contrast, Schuck et al. (2019) reported no relationship between

camouflaging and executive functioning. Similarly, for IQ, Livingston, Colvert, et al. (2019) showed

that greater camouflaging, as measured by the discrepancy method, was related to higher Full-Scale

and Verbal IQ. However, other studies found that camouflaging was unrelated to IQ using either

discrepancy (Corbett et al., 2021; Lai et al., 2017) or self-report methods (Hull et al., 2021).

title: Baseline machine learning prediction of 2-year remission from anxiety, depression, and eating disorders among college students after population-based guided self-help: A secondary analysis of a randomized controlled trial
abstract:
Background: We examined whether machine learning could identify baseline variables that predicted 2-year follow-up prevention and remission from anxiety, depression, and eating disorders following population-based digital cognitive-behavioral therapy guided self-help (D-CBTgsh).

Methods: Undergraduates at risk for or meeting criteria for anxiety, depression, and eating disorders who were randomized to screening+D-CBTgsh were examined. A five-step feature selection pipeline reduced 863 baseline questionnaire items to 37 predictors spanning demographics, mental health problems, treatment motivation, and treatment targets. Six machine learning algorithms were trained and tested using nested cross-validation among students from 13 U.S. colleges (n = 1472) to discriminate who would (versus would not) achieve two-year remission and prevention of panic, generalized anxiety, social anxiety, major depressive, and eating disorders. External validation was conducted in a separate set of 13 U.S. colleges (n = 823) to assess algorithm discrimination, calibration, clinical utility, and feature importance.

Findings: When externally validated, the random forest algorithm outperformed all models, yielding an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of .759 (95% CI = .727-.790) with excellent calibration (integrated calibration index = .086), corresponding to a large effect (d = 0.99) and greater net benefit than benchmark models. Predictors included lower generalized anxiety interference, behavioral avoidance, social fear, social anxiety interference, number of worry topics, fear of observation, depressed mood, perseverative thinking, and binge eating, along with higher feelings of calm/peacefulness, perceived need for treatment, and intention and willingness to seek help. Demographic predictors included being male at birth, Asian, older, and with lower current and past-year body mass index, fewer financial difficulties, and higher parental education.

Interpretation: If implemented on college campuses, this predictive algorithm based on 37 single items created a scalable self-report measure that could be easily adopted to help allocate higher-intensity resources at a population-level.

Funding: ClinicalTrials.gov: NCT04162847.

 MACHINE LEARNING PREDICTION OF TWO-YEAR OUTCOMES                                                   2


                                             Abstract

Background: We examined whether machine learning could identify baseline variables that
predicted 2-year follow-up prevention and remission from anxiety, depression, and eating
disorders following population-based digital cognitive-behavioral therapy guided self-help (D-
CBTgsh).

Methods: Undergraduates at risk for or meeting criteria for anxiety, depression, and eating
disorders who were randomized to screening+D-CBTgsh were examined. A five-step feature
selection pipeline reduced 863 baseline questionnaire items to 37 predictors spanning
demographics, mental health problems, treatment motivation, and treatment targets. Six machine
learning algorithms were trained and tested using nested cross-validation among students from
13 U.S. colleges (n = 1472) to discriminate who would (versus would not) achieve two-year
remission and prevention of panic, generalized anxiety, social anxiety, major depressive, and
eating disorders. External validation was conducted in a separate set of 13 U.S. colleges (n =
823) to assess algorithm discrimination, calibration, clinical utility, and feature importance.

Results: When externally validated, the random forest algorithm outperformed all models,
yielding an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of .759 (95% CI = .727-.790)
with excellent calibration (integrated calibration index = .086), corresponding to a large effect (d
= 0.99) and greater net benefit than benchmark models. Predictors included lower generalized
anxiety interference, behavioral avoidance, social fear, social anxiety interference, number of
worry topics, fear of observation, depressed mood, perseverative thinking, and binge eating,
along with higher feelings of calm/peacefulness, perceived need for treatment, and intention and
willingness to seek help. Demographic predictors included being male at birth, Asian, older, and
with lower current and past-year body mass index, fewer financial difficulties, and higher
parental education.

Conclusions: If implemented on college campuses, this predictive algorithm, based on 37 single
items, created a scalable self-report measure that could be easily adopted to help allocate higher-
intensity resources at a population level.

Funding: ClinicalTrials.gov: NCT04162847.
 MACHINE LEARNING PREDICTION OF TWO-YEAR OUTCOMES                                                      3


                                           Introduction

       Due to rising mental health problems on U.S. college campuses, more efficient

approaches to treatment are needed. About 40-50% of students suffer from anxiety, depression,

and/or eating disorders (EDs).1 These diagnoses often emerge by age 24 and can have lasting

effects on students' health, social relationships, and academic achievements.2,3 However, only

20-40% of students with such disorders receive treatment4-6, a problem compounded by the

difficulty of most colleges to meet the growing demand for services.

       Digital tools, when paired with universal access to mental health screening, hold great

promise to attenuate treatment gaps. Coached digital interventions increase affordability, uptake,

and scalability while demonstrating efficacy for different mental health disorders.7 When

combined with population-based access to screening, those at risk for anxiety, depression, and

EDs can be assessed quickly and immediately access tailored treatment programs within a single

digital platform. In the largest population-based randomized clinical trial of targeted prevention

and intervention among students, Newman et al.8 evaluated the effectiveness of digital cognitive-

behavioral therapy guided self-help, following guideline-based mental health screening with

feedback (screening+D-CBTgsh) relative to screening+referral to college-provided care (rTAU)

for prevention of new disorders and treatment of anxiety, depression, and EDs. Students assigned

to screening+D-CBTgsh had a 3.8% lower prevalence of any disorder at two-year follow-up than

those assigned to rTAU (OR, 0.86; 95% CI, 0.77-0.95). However, 55.5% (1723/3103) did not

achieve non-case status for all anxiety, depression, and EDs. Thus, not all students benefited,

suggesting that more intensive care may have been warranted.

       If students are to be optimally matched with low-cost digital prevention and intervention

tools from the outset, accurately predicting the potential for a given individual to benefit in the
 MACHINE LEARNING PREDICTION OF TWO-YEAR OUTCOMES                                                    4


long term from this type of care is crucial. Most U.S. colleges offer a range of mental health

resources, and deciding between low (digital tools) and high (psychotherapy) intensity treatments

is often based on student preference and/or clinical judgment, both of which have been shown to

be prone to bias and error.9 This is further complicated by no current guidelines for

recommending when coached digital interventions should be considered as a first-line

approach.10 Thus, when students use ineffectual services, they may drop out, experience

worsening symptoms, or try several services, resulting in prolonged disorders, lower chance of

recovery, and higher costs. Accordingly, research is needed to determine whether long-term

prevention and remission of all anxiety, depression, and EDs (or not) from a coached digital

intervention can be detected at baseline. Students predicted to achieve long-term non-case status

may be best matched to a coached digital intervention, whereas those predicted to maintain long-

term case status may require higher-intensity treatments. Preliminary studies on such stratified

approaches to care have demonstrated that they are more effective and cost-effective than

stepped care, for instance, with a 7% difference in depression remission11 — a substantial

difference especially when considered at the population level.12 However, identifying stratifying

variables that bear prognostic information about probability of response remains challenging.13

       The best method for early identification of long-term outcomes of coached digital

interventions has yet to be established but is likely to include machine learning approaches.

Compared to ordinary least squares regression methods, supervised machine learning methods

can aggregate disparate small variable effects to capture complex higher-order interactions and

non-linear associations between predictors and a target outcome. Therefore, characteristics of

students (e.g., sociodemographic, motivational, and clinical variables) that campus counseling

centers routinely collect would be particularly amenable to such an approach. Past efforts to
 MACHINE LEARNING PREDICTION OF TWO-YEAR OUTCOMES                                                   5


develop supervised machine learning algorithms from prior trials of coached digital interventions

have demonstrated preliminary support in using baseline characteristics to predict outcomes,

including in social anxiety disorder (SAD) at 12 weeks14 and 1 year follow-up,15 generalized

anxiety disorder (GAD) at 8 weeks16 and 9-month follow-up,17 panic disorder (PD) at 12

weeks,14 depression at post-treatment,18 2 weeks,19 8 weeks,16 12 weeks,14,20, 9-month follow-

up,17 and EDs after 4 weeks.21 To our knowledge, there have not been any studies that have

either included: 1) college students, 2) a transdiagnostic coached mobile program delivered

following population level access to screening, 3) an extensive set of scalable baseline predictors

across relevant domains, 4) the prevention and remission of anxiety, depression, and EDs as an

outcome, 5) longer-term outcomes (greater than one year), or 6) used external validation to

ensure generalizability.

       To this aim, we developed six supervised machine learning algorithms (logistic

regression, random forest, gradient boosting, extreme gradient boosting, light gradient boosting

machine, categorical boosting) based on data from a population-based randomized controlled

trial to identify students who would (versus would not) achieve non-case status from PD, GAD,

SAD, depression, and EDs at a two-year follow-up after screening+D-CBTgsh.8 To provide a

more nuanced understanding of the specific factors contributing to outcomes and improve the

scalability of our final pool of predictors, we sought to narrow down a limited set of single items

based on full questionnaires assessing demographics, mental health problems, motivation for

treatment, and treatment targets. Predictive algorithms were initially trained and independently

tested on students from 13 colleges that varied by geographic region, enrollment size, sector

(public vs. private), and institutional type (e.g., research-intensive, community college). We then
 MACHINE LEARNING PREDICTION OF TWO-YEAR OUTCOMES                                                   6


externally validated whether the algorithms could be effectively applied across a similar but

separate set of 13 colleges.

                                           Methods

We adhered to the Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual

Prognosis Or Diagnosis (TRIPOD) + AI guidelines (Table S1).22

Study population and data source

Participants were part of a population-based randomized controlled trial across 26 U.S. colleges

that was conducted between October 2019 and December 2023 (ClinicalTrials.gov:

NCT04162847)23, which compared screening+D-CBTgsh with rTAU to prevent and treat

anxiety, depression, and EDs. Participating colleges deferred to Washington University’s

institutional review board. Eligible participants were undergraduates aged 18 years or older who

had clinical levels of or were at risk for self-reported diagnoses of PD, GAD, SAD, depression,

and/or EDs (excluding anorexia nervosa, which requires medical monitoring). Access to a

smartphone was required, and those receiving current psychosocial treatment were excluded.

Students with high suicidal ideation received immediate follow-up but not excluded. They were

enrolled for two years and were asked to complete online assessments at baseline, six-week, six-

month, and two-year follow-up. The original sample included 6,205 students, randomized within

26 colleges to either screening+D-CBTgsh (n=3,103) or rTAU (n=3,102). For the current study,

data were restricted to students in the screening+D-CBTgsh arm.

Coached digital intervention

The D-CBTgsh platform, detailed in the trial protocol23, was hosted by SilverCloud Health and

included programs for anxiety, depression, and EDs. Each program had six to eight evidence-

based modules, lasting approximately 20 minutes, and targeted treatment goals from the start
 MACHINE LEARNING PREDICTION OF TWO-YEAR OUTCOMES                                                    7


(i.e., decreasing avoidance for anxiety, increasing behavioral activation for depression,

decreasing dietary restraint and weight/shape concerns for EDs). If participants did not improve

after six weeks, they were provided a referral to usual care on their campus while still

encouraged to continue with the program. Access to the platform was provided for six months.

Statistical Analysis

Feature Selection, Engineering, and Data Processing

We implemented a five-step feature selection pipeline to evaluate all baseline survey items as

candidate predictors (Figure S1). First, we excluded clinically irrelevant or duplicate survey

items and metadata fields. Second, we removed variables with near-zero variance (threshold =

0.01) to minimize noise and prevent overfitting. Third, we excluded highly correlated predictors

(defined as those with pairwise Pearson correlation coefficients exceeding 0.9) to address

multicollinearity. Fourth, we computed random forest-based feature importance scores for each

predictor and tested top-k sets ranked by importance (k = 10-150, with a step size of 10) to retain

the smallest subset that preserved model performance. Fifth, an expert consensus review

(M.G.N., D.E.W., E.F.C., D.E., C.B.T.) ensured that each predictor could be readily understood

and feasibly collected by clinicians and college administrators. Categorical predictors were one-

hot encoded, and continuous variables were transformed using normalization (z-score).

Baseline predictors

From the initial 863 questionnaire items, our feature selection pipeline yielded 37 predictors

across four domains (Table 1): demographics, mental health problems, treatment motivation, and

treatment targets. Demographic variables (n = 23) included age, sex at birth, race (American

Indian or Alaskan Native, Asian, Black or African American, Multiracial, N

title: AI-Powered Early Intervention for Depression and Anxiety: A Randomized Clinical Trial
abstract:
Importance: Depression and anxiety are prevalent and disabling, yet most individuals receive inadequate or no care. Purpose-built generative AI platforms may offer scalable first-line support, though large-trial evidence remains limited.
Objective: To determine whether Flourish, an AI-powered mental health platform, prevents and reduces depression and anxiety symptoms compared with care-as-usual.
Design: Preregistered, parallel-group, superiority randomized clinical trial conducted in 2025, with assessments at weeks 0, 2, 4, and 6.
Setting: A large North American university.
Participants: A total of 1,137 college students aged 18 years or older was recruited through a research participation system without symptom-based screening and randomized to treatment or care-as-usual. 850 participants (74.6%) completed the final Week 6 follow-up.
Intervention: Participants assigned to treatment received Flourish, which offered self-guided support informed by cognitive behavioral therapy, dialectical behavior therapy, acceptance and commitment therapy, positive psychology, and motivational interviewing, and were instructed to use it at least twice weekly. Participants assigned to care-as-usual retained access to usual mental health resources.
Main Outcomes and Measures: Preregistered primary outcomes were depression (Patient Health Questionnaire-2) and anxiety symptoms (Generalized Anxiety Disorder-2), assessed biweekly.
Results: Among 1,137 participants, mean (SD) age was 20.77 (5.34) years, and 853 (75.0%) were female. Symptom trajectories were more favorable in treatment (vs control) for depression (condition × time: β = −0.03; 95% CI, −0.05 to −0.01; P = .006) and anxiety (β = −0.04; 95% CI, −0.06 to −0.01; P = .001). Among participants with elevated baseline symptom severity, treatment increased rates of meaningful change for depressive symptoms (52.3% vs 31.7%; risk ratio [RR], 1.65 [95% CI, 1.16-2.34]; number needed to treat [NNT], 4.9 [95% CI, 2.9-14.8]) and anxiety symptoms (50.6% vs 38.1%; RR, 1.33 [95% CI, 1.05-1.68]; NNT, 8.0 [95% CI, 4.4-44.5]). Participants with low baseline symptoms showed less symptom worsening at 6 weeks. No adverse events attributable to the intervention were identified.
Conclusions and Relevance: This trial demonstrates that a purpose-built generative AI platform can serve as a scalable first-line mental health tool, effective for prevention and early intervention of depression and anxiety symptoms in an unscreened general population.
Trial Registration: ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT06668532 (https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06668532).

title: Is combined antidepressant medication (ADM) and psychotherapy better than either monotherapy at preventing suicide attempts and other psychiatric serious adverse events for depressed patients? A rare events meta-analysis
abstract: Background: Antidepressant medication (ADM)-only, psychotherapy-only, and their combination are the first-line treatment options for major depressive disorder (MDD). Previous meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) established that psychotherapy and combined treatment were superior to ADM-only for MDD treatment remission or response. The current meta-analysis extended previous ones by determining the comparative efficacy of ADM-only, psychotherapy-only, and combined treatment on suicide attempts and other serious psychiatric adverse events (i.e., psychiatric emergency department (ED) visit, psychiatric hospitalization, and/or suicide death; SAEs). Methods: Peto odds ratios (ORs) and their 95% confidence intervals were computed from the present random effects meta-analysis. Thirty-four relevant RCTs were included. Results: Psychotherapy-only was stronger than combined treatment (1.9% v. 3.7%; OR = 1.96 [1.20, 3.20], p = .012) and ADM-only (3.0% v. 5.6%; OR = 0.45 [0.30, 0.67], p = .001) in decreasing the likelihood of SAEs in the primary and trim-and-fill sensitivity analyses. Combined treatment was better than ADM-only in reducing the probability of SAEs (6.0% v. 8.7%; OR = 0.74 [0.56, 0.96], p = .029), but this comparative efficacy finding was non-significant in the sensitivity analyses. Subgroup analyses revealed the advantage of psychotherapy-only over combined treatment and ADM-only for reducing SAE risk among children and adolescents and the benefit of combined treatment over ADM-only among adults. Conclusions: Overall, psychotherapy and combined treatment outperformed ADM-only in reducing the likelihood of SAEs, perhaps by conferring strategies to enhance reasons for living. Plausibly, psychotherapy should be prioritized for high-risk youths and combined treatment for high-risk adults with MDD.

                                             Abstract

       Background: Antidepressant medication (ADM)-only, psychotherapy-only, and their

combination are the first-line treatment options for major depressive disorder (MDD). Previous

meta-analyses of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) established that psychotherapy and

combined treatment were superior to ADM-only for MDD treatment remission or response. The

current meta-analysis extended previous ones by determining the comparative efficacy of ADM-

only, psychotherapy-only, and combined treatment on suicide attempts and other serious

psychiatric adverse events (i.e., psychiatric emergency department (ED) visit, psychiatric

hospitalization, and/or suicide death; SAEs). Methods: Peto odds ratios (ORs) and their 95%

confidence intervals were computed from the present random effects meta-analysis. Thirty-four

relevant RCTs were included. Results: Psychotherapy-only was stronger than combined

treatment (1.9% v. 3.7%; OR = 1.96 [1.20, 3.20], p = .012) and ADM-only (3.0% v. 5.6%; OR =

0.45 [0.30, 0.67], p = .001) in decreasing the likelihood of SAEs in the primary and trim-and-fill

sensitivity analyses. Combined treatment was better than ADM-only in reducing the probability

of SAEs (6.0% v. 8.7%; OR = 0.74 [0.56, 0.96], p = .029), but this comparative efficacy finding

was non-significant in the sensitivity analyses. Subgroup analyses revealed the advantage of

psychotherapy-only over combined treatment and ADM-only for reducing SAE risk among

children and adolescents and the benefit of combined treatment over ADM-only among adults.

Conclusions: Overall, psychotherapy and combined treatment outperformed ADM-only in

reducing the likelihood of SAEs, perhaps by conferring strategies to enhance reasons for living.

Plausibly, psychotherapy should be prioritized for high-risk youths and combined treatment for

high-risk adults with MDD.

       Keywords: suicide attempt; serious psychiatric adverse events; psychotherapy;

antidepressant medication; combined treatment; comparative efficacy research
                                                                                                    4


Is Combined Antidepressant Medication and Psychotherapy Better Than Either Monotherapy at

   Preventing Suicide Attempts and Other Psychiatric Serious Adverse Events for Depressed

                              Patients? A Rare Events Meta-Analysis

       Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a common mental disorder entwined with impaired

functioning in academic, occupational, and social domains (Kupferberg, Bicks, & Hasler, 2016)

and compromised physical health (Zainal & Newman, 2022) and quality of life (Hohls, Konig, &

Hajek, 2023). Economically, MDD imposes a hefty financial burden on government budgets due

to substantial healthcare utilization costs, increased absenteeism and presenteeism, and reduced

work productivity (Greenberg et al., 2021). One of the most devastating consequences of MDD

is mortality due to a suicide attempt and other related causes (Chiu et al., 2023). Determining

effective treatments for MDD to reduce the risk for or prevent suicide attempts and other serious

psychiatric adverse events is thus essential.

       The first-line treatments for MDD include psychotherapy-only, especially cognitive-

behavioral and related therapies, antidepressant medication (ADM)-only, and combined

treatment (GBD 2017 Disease and Injury Incidence and Prevalence Collaborators, 2018). Meta-

analyses of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that examined MDD treatment response or

remission rates as primary outcomes of interest showed that combined treatment conferred a

substantially stronger aggregate effect than ADM-only, psychotherapy-only had a better

aggregate effect than ADM-only, and the aggregate effect between combined treatment and

psychotherapy-only was not different (Cuijpers et al., 2023; Furukawa et al., 2021; Karyotaki et

al., 2016). Collectively, such findings might translate to the possibility that MDD treatments that

comprise psychotherapy, especially those that embody cognitive-behavioral and related theories,
                                                                                                   5


would be associated with reduced risk of suicide attempts and associated serious psychiatric

adverse events.

       Only six meta-analyses have determined if psychotherapy or ADM monotherapies

mitigated the risk for suicide attempts and related serious psychiatric adverse events. Five recent

meta-analyses showed that depressed patients randomized to psychotherapy-only, such as

cognitive-behavioral therapies (Calati & Courtet, 2016; Gotzsche & Gotzsche, 2017), dialectical

behavioral therapy (Kothgassner, Robinson, Goreis, Ougrin, & Plener, 2020), psychodynamic-

oriented therapies (Briggs et al., 2019), and suicide-focused psychotherapies (Riblet, Shiner,

Young-Xu, & Watts, 2017), were less likely to engage in suicide attempt, self-harm behaviors,

and encounter other serious psychiatric adverse events compared to their counterparts assigned to

treatment-as-usual or waitlist. However, another recent meta-analysis showed that the probability

for depressed patients randomized to receive ADM to experience any of these serious psychiatric

adverse events was not different from those who received placebos (Braun, Bschor, Franklin, &

Baethge, 2016). Further, a major limitation of these meta-analyses was the exclusion of RCTs

that examined the comparative effects of psychotherapy-only, ADM-only, and combined

treatment.

       Accordingly, the present exploratory meta-analysis evaluated the comparative effects of

psychotherapy-only, ADM-only, and combined treatment on suicide attempts and other serious

psychiatric adverse events. Such serious psychiatric adverse events included psychiatric

emergency department (ED) visits, psychiatric hospitalization, and/or suicide deaths among

depressed patients. Knowing what treatment option works best to decrease the likelihood of

suicide attempts and other serious psychiatric adverse events at the aggregate level could aid

with successful suicide prevention efforts for depressed patients.
                                                                                                         6


                                              Method

Procedures and eligibility criteria

        The present meta-analysis protocol was preregistered on the Open Science Framework

(OSF; https://osf.io/c7z8d) and the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews

(PROSPERO; CRD42023443819). The conventional approach instructed by Cochrane was

applied for the analyses, and the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews

and Meta-Analyses) guidelines were followed in reporting the methods and results (Figure 1;

Moher et al., 2015; Page et al., 2021). The inclusion criteria included clinical trials that reported

on number of patients with suicide attempts and/or other serious psychiatric adverse events (i.e.,

psychiatric ED visit, psychiatric hospitalization, or suicide death) at post-treatment in the

intervention and control arms. Baseline randomization to psychotherapy-only, ADM-only, or

combined treatment and recruitment of patients with a primary diagnosis of a depressive disorder

was required. Following best practices, relevant RCTs that reported zero suicide attempts and

serious psychiatric adverse events in the treatment and control arms were included, given that

this is a more conservative method and enhances the generalizability of the results (Friedrich,

Adhikari, & Beyene, 2007). Exclusion criteria included studies that used waitlist, treatment-as-

usual, placebo, or another active control (e.g., ketamine) as a comparator, nonrandomized

controlled trials, and RCTs that did not recruit patients with a primary diagnosis of a depressive

disorder. Although challenges in utilizing RCTs to examine rare outcomes such as suicide

attempts and other serious psychiatric adverse events were acknowledged, RCTs can provide

essential information on the comparative risk of such serious psychiatric adverse events when

evaluating two or more treatment options. Nonetheless, the present meta-analysis centered on

RCTs given that it is the gold standard design to determine comparative effectiveness (or
                                                                                                     7


efficacy; Rosen, Manor, Engelhard, & Zucker, 2006), and an adequate number of RCTs (i.e., 34

eligible RCTs; Figure 1) have examined how psychotherapy-only, ADM-only, and combined

treatment might reduce the risk of suicide attempts and other serious psychiatric adverse events.

Table 1 summarizes the 34 eligible RCTs in the present meta-analysis and relays information

about the number of suicide attempts, psychiatric ED visits, psychiatric hospitalization, and/or

suicide deaths extracted from each RCT.

Study selection

       Cochrane Library, Google Scholar, PubMed, and ScienceDirect were searched from the

inception of each database until June 30, 2023, to identify published articles evaluating our

research question. The following search terms and Boolean terms were used: ("suicide attempt")

AND ("antidepressant") AND ("psychotherapy") AND ("trial") AND ("depression") AND

("serious adverse events"). A highly sensitive search approach that prioritized the detection of

RCTs in these online databases was used (Riblet et al., 2017). Table S1 in the online

supplementary materials (OSM) details blow-by-blow how each study measured and reported the

rates of suicide attempt(s) and/or other serious psychiatric adverse event(s) in each arm.

Intervention arms

       Concerning psychotherapy type, the most common face-to-face psychotherapy was

individual cognitive-behavioral therapies (CBT; n = 11, 32.4%), followed by individual,

interpersonal psychotherapy (n = 5, 14.7%), group CBT (n = 1, 2.9%), and psychotherapy

without a specified theoretical orientation (n = 1, 2.9%). The remaining 16 studies (47.1%) did

not use psychotherapy-only as a comparator. Regarding ADM type, most studies did not specify

the ADM type (n = 15, 44.1%) followed by selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs; i.e.,

citalopram, fluoxetine, imipramine, sertraline; n = 12, 35.3%), and venlafaxine (a serotonin-
                                                                                                     8


norepinephrine reuptake inhibitory; SNRI; n = 1, 2.9%). The remaining 6 studies (17.6%) did not

use ADM-only as a comparator.

Data analyses

       Using the Peto approach (Bradburn, Deeks, Berlin, & Russell Localio, 2007; Lane,

2013), summary odds ratios (ORs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) and p-values were

computed. Standard meta-analytic techniques (e.g., calculating risk ratio) are not recommended

to determine rare outcomes such as suicide attempts and other serious psychiatric adverse events

(i.e., psychiatric ED visits, psychiatric hospitalization, and/or suicide death; Bradburn et al.,

2007; Lane, 2013). The Peto approach is an appropriate and robust alternative option for meta-

analyzing data when event rates are less than 1% (Bradburn et al., 2007; Lane, 2013). No

continuity correction for treatment arms with zero negative outcome events was applied since

prior research showed that it would bias estimates with the Peto approach (Friedrich et al., 2007;

Sweeting, Sutton, & Lambert, 2004).

       The rmeta R package was used to conduct these random effects meta-analyses for rare

events and to generate forest plots. The conventional definition of an OR was used to interpret

the present findings (i.e., likelihood of the occurrence of suicide attempts and other serious

psychiatric adverse events v. likelihood of the non-occurrence of suicide attempts and other

serious psychiatric adverse events in the intervention arm v. comparator arm). An OR value < 1

indicated that the relative odds of suicide attempts and other serious psychiatric adverse events

were lower in the treatment arm v. comparator arm, and the opposite was true with an OR value >

1. The OR was considered to be statistically significant if the 95% CIs did not cross 1 (Szumilas,

2010). Standard thresholds of Cochrane's Q p-value < .10 and I2 > 50% were used to determine

between-study heterogeneity (Deeks, Higgins, & Altman, 2008).
                                                                                                      9


           Contour-enhanced funnel plots were created to evaluate publication bias to determine if

any funnel plot asymmetry indicated substantial publication bias (Peters, Sutton, Jones, Abrams,

& Rushton, 2008). Moreover, Harbord's modified assessment of small study effects was used to

test for publication bias for binary outcomes (Harbord, Egger, & Sterne, 2006; Peters, Sutton,

Jones, Abrams, & Rushton, 2006) with the meta R package (Balduzzi, Rucker, & Schwarzer,

2019). This approach utilized a modified linear regression analysis to detect significant funnel

plot asymmetry that would denote publicat

title: Emerging Stressors and Adolescent Depression and Anxiety: Associations and Cumulative Burden Among Kenyan Adolescents
abstract: Depression and anxiety among young people are rising globally, and one proposed explanation is that the stressor environment of adolescence has changed. Emerging stressors—person-level exposures born of connectivity, technological change, and collective crises—are increasingly linked to adolescent distress in high-income countries; sub-Saharan Africa, where these exposures are spreading fastest, remains almost entirely unstudied. We examined whether emerging stressors across two domains—digital (problematic social-media use, online appearance comparison) and global (climate-change anxiety, pandemic-related stress, exposure to ethnic-political violence)—were associated with depression (PHQ-8) and anxiety (GAD-7) among Kenyan adolescents in two cross-sectional school studies (N = 5,592; Mage = 15.9 years; 49.8% female; 74 schools), and whether risk accumulated as stressors co-occurred; exploratory analyses tested body-esteem mediation and moderation by gender and structural disadvantage. After establishing scalar measurement invariance across samples, symptoms were regressed on each stressor within its study, using covariate-adjusted linear and logistic mixed-effects models with school random intercepts, estimated in 20 multiply imputed datasets with false-discovery-rate correction; we report standardized βs (per SD) and odds ratios for clinically elevated symptoms (≥ 10). Cumulative burden was the count of co-occurring stressors (top-tertile exposure). Clinically elevated depression (28.1%) and anxiety (24.5%) were common and did not differ between samples. Every stressor was independently associated with both outcomes (βs = 0.20–0.36; odds ratios = 1.39–1.92 per SD), with climate-change anxiety strongest (βs = 0.33–0.36). The odds of clinically elevated symptoms roughly doubled with each additional co-occurring stressor (odds ratios = 1.86–2.32), monotonically, in both samples. Indirect effects through body esteem were significant but small (0.04–0.09) relative to direct paths (~0.30), and associations were not moderated by gender, socioeconomic status, or family structure. Emerging stressors are consistently associated with adolescent depression and anxiety in Kenya, and their burden accumulates as exposures co-occur. Future studies should test longitudinal associations and all stressor domains.

title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a 14-Day Mindfulness Ecological Momentary Intervention (MEMI) for Generalized Anxiety Disorder
abstract: BACKGROUND: Little is known about whether brief mindfulness ecological momentary interventions (MEMI) yield clinically beneficial effects. This gap exists despite the rapid growth of smartphone mindfulness applications. Specifically, no prior brief MEMI has targeted generalized anxiety disorder (GAD). Moreover, although theories propose that MEMI can boost executive functioning (EF), they have largely gone untested. Thus, this randomized controlled trial (RCT) aimed to address these gaps by assessing the efficacy of a 14-day smartphone MEMI (vs. self-monitoring placebo (SMP)). METHOD: Participants with GAD were randomly assigned to either condition (68 MEMI, 42 SMP). MEMI participants exercised multiple core mindfulness strategies and were instructed to practice mindfulness continually. Comparatively, SMP participants were prompted to practice self-monitoring and were not taught any mindfulness strategies. All prompts occurred five times a day for 14 consecutive days. Participants completed self-reports and neuropsychological assessments at baseline, post-treatment, and 1-month follow-up (1MFU). Piecewise hierarchical linear modeling analyses were conducted. RESULTS: MEMI (vs. SMP) produced greater pre-1MFU reductions in GAD severity and perseverative cognitions (between-group |d|=0.393–0.394) and stronger improvements in trait mindfulness and performance-based inhibition (|d|=0.280–0.303). Further, MEMI (vs. SMP) led to more considerable pre-post-treatment reductions in state-level depression and anxiety and more mindfulness gains (|d|=0.50–1.13). Overall, between-treatment effects were stronger at pre-1MFU than pre-post-treatment for trait-level than state-level treatment outcome measures. CONCLUSIONS: Preliminary findings suggest that the beneficial effect of an unguided brief MEMI to target pathological worry, trait mindfulness, and EF is modest yet potentially meaningful. Other theoretical and clinical implications were discussed.

title: Curiosity Helps: Growth In Need for Cognition Bidirectionally Predicts Future Reduction In Anxiety and Depression Symptoms Across 10 Years
abstract: Theorists (Cacioppo, Petty, Feinstein, & Jarvis, 1996) have proposed that persons with diminished motivation to actively and systematically seek out, consider, and think about corrective information (i.e., low need for cognition; NFC) are vulnerable to encounter elevated anxiety and depression across long periods. Simultaneously, based on the scar theory (Allemand, Grünenfelder-Steiger, & Flückiger, 2018; Schmidt, Lerew, & Joiner, 2000), increased depression and anxiety could relate to lower trait NFC across time, by compromising analytical thinking, goal-directed pursuits, and reality testing skills. However, most prior studies used cross-sectional designs which preclude causal inferences. In addition, longitudinal studies on this topic thus far applied ordinary least squares regression or latent growth curve models, which permits between-person but not within-person inferences. Building on prior work, we used two advanced longitudinal structural equation modeling (SEM) techniques to test the within-person and between-person relations between NFC and depression and anxiety symptoms (DAS) measured every year, across 10 years. Random intercept-cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM; Hamaker, Kuiper, & Grasman, 2015) was used to test level-to-future level cross-lagged relations, whereas bivariate dual latent change score (BLCS; Grimm & Ram, 2018) modeling was conducted to examine change-to-future change associations. Further, both BLCS and RI-CLPM adjusted for baseline scores, prior lagged effects, regression to the mean, and between-person variances, as well as minimized measurement error. Participants comprised community-dwelling adults (n = 6,750) in the Longitudinal Internet Survey Study (LISS) (Scherpenzeel, Das, Ester, & Kaczmirek, 2010). RI-CLPM showed that within persons, lower prior level of NFC was significantly related to higher level of NFC at the future adjacent time-point (d = -0.852), and vice versa (d = -0.498) over 10 years. Similarly, the BLCS model demonstrated that within persons, greater 1-year reduction in NFC was notably associated with larger 1-year increase in DAS at the next time-lag across 10 years (d = -0.631), and conversely (d = -0.519). Moreover, within the RI-CLPM, a significant between-person cross-sectional result emerged, such that higher DAS was associated with lower trait NFC (d = -1.738). The results suggested that targeting NFC may treat or prevent the emergence of depression and anxiety disorders. For example, cognitive behavioral therapy techniques (e.g., cognitive reframing, behavioral activation) can be augmented to enhance NFC by helping patients engage in self-talk in curious ways as well as to connect them with work, hobbies, and activities that instill joy and excitement. Other theoretical and practical implications for the clinical and educational psychological sciences were discussed.

 Abstract


           CURIOSITY AND MENTAL HEALTH                                                                      1


                                                        Abstract

                  Introduction: Decreased motivational tendency to seek out, engage in, and enjoy

           investing in complex cognitive processes in a sustained manner (need for cognition; NFC) may

           be a predictor and consequence of heightened anxiety and depression symptoms (ADS).

           However, the majority of investigations on this topic have been cross-sectional, which hinders

           causal inferences. Methods: The current study thus determined the within-person relations

           between NFC and ADS by using random intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CLPM) and

           bivariate dual latent change score (BLCS) approaches to separate between- and within-person

           effects. RI-CLPM and BLCS also present with advantages of adjusting for regression to the

           mean, baseline scores, autoregressive and lagged effects, and minimizing measurement error.

           Community-dwelling adults (n = 6750) completed the Mental Health Inventory-5 and trait-level

           NFC scales approximately every one year across 10 years. Results: RI-CLPM showed that

           within persons, lower level of NFC predicted higher future level of ADS, and vice versa (d = -

           0.852 to -0.498). Likewise, BLCS demonstrated that within persons, smaller change in NFC

           forecasted larger subsequent increase in ADS, and conversely (d = -0.631 to -0.519). Findings

           remained after adjusting for socio-demographic covariates. Conclusion: Consistent with

           theories, findings suggested that the within-person level-to-future level and change-to-future

           change relations among NFC and ADS were bi-directional and negative, with moderate-to-large

           effect sizes. Targeting NFC may treat or prevent the emergence of depression and anxiety

           disorders. Such efforts may include augmenting or personalizing evidence-based cognitive

           behavioral therapeutic strategies for individuals with or at-risk for heightened ADS.

title: The Relationship Between Trauma Type, Coping Style, and Posttraumatic Symptomatology: A Retrospective Online Study
abstract:
Background: Previous research has shown that interpersonal trauma is associated with higher rates of posttraumatic stress symptoms, with coping responses playing a vital role in individual differences in symptom presentation. The present online study examined differential associations between trauma type, coping styles, and trauma-related symptomatology to deepen understanding of variation in trauma-related symptomatology.
Methods: The sample included N = 300 participants who reported a traumatic life event within the past 18 months, assessed with the Life Events Checklist for DSM-5 (LEC-5). Events were classified into interpersonal and non-interpersonal trauma. Participants completed retrospective questions assessing posttraumatic coping strategies, current levels of posttraumatic stress symptoms, depression, anxiety, stress, and life satisfaction.
Results: The present study reports secondary analyses. Correlational analyses within both trauma groups revealed positive relationships between avoidant coping and trauma-related symptoms. Group comparisons revealed higher levels of trauma-related symptoms, lower life satisfaction, and greater use of avoidant coping strategies in the interpersonal trauma group compared to the non-interpersonal trauma group. Furthermore, avoidant coping explained a significant proportion of variance in posttraumatic stress symptoms within both trauma groups, and trauma type itself accounted for significant unique variance in posttraumatic stress symptoms across the entire sample. Finally, exploratory mediation analyses provided evidence consistent with a partial mediation of the association between trauma type and posttraumatic stress symptoms.
Conclusions: These findings suggest that avoidant coping may be an important factor in understanding differences in trauma-related symptoms across trauma types. The results also point to potential intervention and prevention targets that should be evaluated in longitudinal research.

 PREPRINT: TRAUMA TYPE, COPING, AND SYMPTOMATOLOGY                                               5

       The Relationship Between Trauma Type, Coping Style, and Posttraumatic

                      Symptomatology: A Retrospective Online Study

                                         1. Introduction

       Most people will experience at least one traumatic event in their lifetime (Benjet et al.,

2016; Kilpatrick et al., 2013). However, not everyone will develop a posttraumatic stress

disorder (PTSD) (Breslau, 2009; Kessler et al., 2017; Stein et al., 2014). This divergence

raises the question of which factors increase the likelihood of severe psychological injury

after a traumatic experience. Interpersonal trauma, defined as an event in which an individual

is personally assaulted or violated by another person, regardless of their prior relationship

(Lilly & Valdez, 2012), has been consistently associated with higher rates of PTSD compared

to non-interpersonal trauma (e.g., natural disasters or traffic accidents) (Breslau et al., 1998;

Forbes et al., 2014; Thomas et al., 2021). Further, interpersonal trauma has been found to be

positively related to symptom frequency and severity in a meta-analysis of PTSD

epidemiology in children and adolescents (Alisic et al., 2014). In addition to heightened

PTSD symptoms, interpersonal trauma has also been linked to elevated levels of depression

(Fowler et al., 2013; Miu et al., 2017), anxiety (McMillan & Asmundson, 2016), general

psychopathology (Hetzel-Riggin & Roby, 2013), negative emotions (Amstadter & Vernon,

2008), difficulties in emotion regulation (Ehring & Quack, 2010), trauma-specific rumination

(Preston et al., 2021), and lower remission rates (Chapman et al., 2012).

       Coping responses play a pivotal role in explaining differences in symptomatology

across different trauma types. The transactional model of stress and coping (Lazarus &

Folkman, 1984) defines coping as the response to the evaluation of a stressor, followed by the

subsequent evaluation of available resources. It proposes two initial types of coping: problem-

focused coping, which aims to solve the problem, and emotion-focused coping, which

involves managing the emotional responses. In terms of their relationships with trauma-

related psychopathology, problem-focused coping is typically adaptive, while emotion-
https://osf.io/7xbcz/overview?view_only=4c6ce3197d5e493aa03c1355d1d1cea2
Preprint v3.0 02.07.2026
 PREPRINT: TRAUMA TYPE, COPING, AND SYMPTOMATOLOGY                                                  6

focused coping is often maladaptive and associated with increased PTSD symptomatology

(e.g., Folkman & Lazarus, 1988; Gil, 2005; Studley & Chung, 2015). However, the specific

pattern of results seems to depend on the conceptualization of the emotion-focused coping

type, since this type can encompass both active and avoidant coping strategies. To illustrate,

Viana Machado et al. (2020) found a negative correlation between posttraumatic stress

symptoms and emotion-focused coping when the latter was limited to non-avoidant strategies.

In line with these and other findings, Carver et al. (1989) proposed a coping model

comprising three coping styles: problem-focused, emotion-focused, and

dysfunctional/avoidant coping. Importantly, avoidant coping also constitutes a core

component in other conceptual frameworks of coping (Roth & Cohen, 1986) and has been

shown to play a critical role empirically. Specifically, avoidant coping has been found to

mediate the relationship between trauma-related shame (Tipsword et al., 2022), negative

social reactions to intimate partner violence (Schackner et al., 2021), and peritraumatic

dissociation (Pacella et al., 2011) with posttraumatic stress symptoms across different trauma

samples.

       Taken together, the literature underscores the central importance of examining how

trauma type and coping styles contribute to trauma-related symptomatology. While much is

known about the associations between posttraumatic symptoms, trauma type, and coping

style, this study integrates these factors within a single analytical approach. Specifically, it

examines differences in coping styles among individuals exposed to interpersonal versus non-

interpersonal trauma, and considers their joint interrelations with posttraumatic

symptomatology. This integrated approach is important because it allows a more nuanced

understanding of how trauma type and coping processes may jointly relate to symptom

severity within the same analytical framework, rather than in isolation. To address these

questions, the present study reports secondary analyses of an online study in which

participants completed retrospective questions assessing posttraumatic coping, along with
