measures of current symptomatology. The primary aim was to compare individuals who had

experienced either interpersonal or non-interpersonal trauma on current posttraumatic stress

symptoms (assessed with the Posttraumatic Stress Disorder Checklist for DSM-5; PCL-5;

Weathers, Litz et al., 2013), current levels of depression, anxiety, and stress (assessed with the

short version of the Depression Anxiety Stress Scale; DASS-21; Lovibond & Lovibond,

1995), current life satisfaction (assessed with the Satisfaction with Life Scale; SWLS; Diener

et al., 1985), and posttraumatic coping style (problem-focused, emotion-focused, avoidant;

assessed with the Coping Orientation to Problems Experienced Inventory; Brief-COPE;

Carver, 1997). We expected to find positive correlations between avoidant coping and

trauma-related symptoms, and negative correlations with life satisfaction, for both trauma

groups. Further, we hypothesized to find higher rates of trauma-related symptoms, lower

levels of life satisfaction, and higher levels of maladaptive coping styles after interpersonal

compared to non-interpersonal trauma. Additionally, we anticipated coping style to be related

with posttraumatic stress symptoms within both trauma groups, and trauma type to be related

with posttraumatic stress symptoms across these groups. Finally, via exploratory analyses, we

expected avoidant coping to statistically mediate the relationship between trauma type and

posttraumatic stress symptoms.

2.1 Participants and Procedures

       Participants were recruited online via the universities' websites and via social media,

as well as offline through flyers posted in various locations to reach eligible participants (e.g.,

at the university's outpatient treatment center for psychotherapy or in self-help groups). The

sample included participants aged 18 or older who were fluent in German and had

experienced a DSM-5 traumatic life event within the past 18 months. Initially, participants

with a current psychiatric disease were excluded from participation. However, to ensure a

more representative sample for examining posttraumatic factors associated with posttraumatic

title: Restrict for Health or for Thinness? Liking but Not Approach Bias Differentiates Orthorexic Eating Behavior From Anorexia Nervosa in Responses to High-Caloric Healthy vs. High-Caloric Unhealthy Foods
abstract:
Objective: Individuals with high levels of orthorexic eating behavior (OR) show a (pathological) fixation on healthy eating, sharing characteristics with individuals with anorexia nervosa (AN). This raised debate over whether orthorexia nervosa constitutes a distinct disorder. Distinctions may lie in preference for and approach behavior toward foods differing in calorie content and healthiness. We expected reduced liking and weaker approach biases in AN and OR compared with controls; specifically for high-caloric foods in AN and for unhealthy foods in OR.
Methods: Self-reported AN (N = 35), OR (high scores on the Düsseldorf Orthorexia Scale but no AN; N = 40), and controls (no eating disorder history, N = 101) completed liking ratings and an Approach-Avoidance Task with food stimuli varying in calorie content (high vs. low) and, for high-calorie items, healthiness (healthy vs. unhealthy). 
Results: Compared to controls, AN reported lower liking for all high-caloric foods, while OR reported lower liking only for high-caloric, unhealthy foods. Accordingly, OR also liked high-caloric healthy foods more than AN. AN showed weaker approach bias than controls (mainly driven by high-caloric unhealthy foods). OR descriptively lay between both other groups, with no significant differences in approach bias to either.
Conclusion: Explicit liking ratings distinguished AN from OR aligning with their respective motivation to reduce high-caloric versus unhealthy food intake. Reduced approach bias was specific to AN, possibly reflecting rapid engagement of approach-avoidance responses to easy-to-categorize high-caloric unhealthy foods. Contrary, health-focused processing in OR may require slower deliberations that are not captured by Approach-Avoidance Tasks.

title: Suicidal Behaviour in Adolescents with Affective Disorders: A Study in a Crisis Intervention Unit (CIU)
abstract: Background: Suicidal behaviour is a critical mental health issue in the adolescent population, often linked to serious emotional problems that leave survivors vulnerable to future risk. Psychological crises in adolescence are primarily associated with relational conflicts, with emotional crises involving depression or anxiety significantly increasing suicidal risk. Objective: This study aimed to evaluate the rates of suicidal behaviour in adolescents undergoing emotional crises and explore their association with psychiatric diagnoses and demographic factors in Ecuador. Methods: An observational, correlational study using a quantitative approach was conducted. Data from 252 adolescents admitted to the Crisis Intervention Unit at the Institute of Neurosciences in Guayaquil, Ecuador, between 2011 and 2023 were analysed. Hospitalisation frequencies by year, gender, and associated psychiatric diagnoses were assessed. Data were obtained from each patient's unified clinical history. Results: Seventy-five percent of the patients were female, with 48% aged 17–18 years. Most adolescents were single (98%), and 86% had a secondary education level. The most frequent diagnosis was a depressive episode (73.4%), with 156 patients presenting suicidal ideation. Cutting and ingestion of psychotropic drugs were the most common suicide methods, predominantly among females. Logistic regression showed that a combination of an eating disorder and a depressive episode was associated with a lower likelihood of suicidal ideation or attempts. Conclusions: The high frequency of suicidal behaviour in adolescent females aged 16–18 years underscores the need for targeted prevention programs addressing emotional crises and stress management in this high-risk group.

                                               Abstract

Background: Suicidal behaviour is a critical mental health issue in the adolescent

population, often linked to serious emotional problems that leave survivors vulnerable to

future risk. Psychological crises in adolescence are primarily associated with relational

conflicts, with emotional crises involving depression or anxiety significantly increasing

suicidal risk. Objective: This study aimed to evaluate the rates of suicidal behaviour in

adolescents undergoing emotional crises and explore their association with psychiatric

diagnoses and demographic factors in Ecuador. Methods: An observational, correlational

history. Results: Seventy-five percent of the patients were female, with 48% aged 17–18

years. Most adolescents were single (98%), and 86% had a secondary education level. The

Keywords: affective disorders, suicidal behaviour, adolescence, depressive episode, anxiety

crisis, suicide attempt, suicidal ideation.
                                         Introduction

       Suicidal behaviour is a complex, multifaceted, multidimensional, and multicausal

phenomenon [1,2]. According to O'Connor et al. [3], suicidal behaviour is defined as a set of

behaviours that develop through motivational and volitional phases, including ideation and

planning. The integrated motivational-volitional model of suicidal behaviour combines

classical models, such as the interpersonal theory of suicide, with more modern theoretical

frameworks into a single process that establishes phases and pathways to suicide.

       In the pre-motivational phase, predisposing biological factors, life events, and

environmental or sociocultural/economic conditions act as drivers. In the motivational phase,

suicidal ideation develops, with mediating factors such as hopelessness and feelings of

entrapment playing a key role [4]. Finally, volitional moderators such as attitudes towards

suicide, access to media, and previous suicide attempts determine the transition from suicidal

thoughts to action [4].

       Attempted suicide occurs much more frequently than completed suicide, and suicidal

ideation is the most common presentation among individuals who die by suicide [5]. The

manifestations of suicidal behaviour can vary in intensity, control, duration, lethality,

impulsivity, and functionality [6]. Factors such as age, sex, educational level, and region of

residence, along with cultural differences, can generate notable variations in its

manifestations [5]. Women attempt suicide three times more often than men, but men die by

suicide three times more frequently than women, with some exceptions in large, multicultural

countries [5].

       Suicidal behaviour is one of the leading causes of death among young people aged 15

to 19 worldwide [8], making it essential to understand the factors that lead to attempted

suicide in order to develop effective prevention strategies. Previous suicidal behaviour is

usually the most common antecedent in individuals who die by suicide, with current
 estimates ranging between 25% and 50% or 40% and 60%, depending on the country and

region [9,10]. A systematic review of European studies found that 16% of cases repeated

suicide attempts within a year, with non-fatal outcomes, while only 2% achieved a fatal

outcome. Over time, the percentage of deaths by suicide increases by approximately 7% [11].

The United Kingdom presents particular trends in this regard [12].

        However, high fatality rates may not always be associated with a history of overt or

covert suicidal ideation or previous attempts [13]. This is illustrated by a study of 75

adolescents who had attempted suicide, where 74.7% had no prior history of suicidal

behaviour, 18.7% had attempted it once, and 6.7% had made two or more attempts [14,15].

        In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death globally among people aged 15

to 29. More than 1.5 million adolescents and young adults aged 10 to 24 died by suicide,

equivalent to over 4,000 deaths per day [16,17]. Data from the World Health Organization

(WHO) indicate that almost one million suicides occurred globally in 2019, resulting in more

deaths from suicide than from wars, homicides, and chronic non-communicable diseases [18].

        Hospitalisation rates for suicide attempts in adolescents remained stable between 2017

and 2019, ranging from 7.9% to 11% [19]. The use of psychotropic drugs is the most

common method of attempted suicide, leading to hospitalisation in 67.7% of cases [20].

Adverse social determinants of health are also associated with increased risks of self-harm

[21].

        A history of previous psychiatric disorders increases the risk of suicidal behaviour

[22], as do academic and social pressures, hormonal changes, and genetic factors [23]. Some

studies report the presence of prior mental disorders in up to 90% of cases involving

individuals who died by suicide, with depression and emotional dysregulation present in 40%

of those patients [13,24].
        Half of the completed suicides in Ecuador involve young people under 30 years of age

[25]. Suicide currently accounts for 1.7% of annual deaths in the country. Regional data

indicate that, over the past twelve months, 4.5% of adolescents attempted to end their lives,

7.5% made a suicide attempt plan, and 14.2% experienced suicidal ideation [25–28]. During

crises, the relationship between depression, anxiety, and suicidal behaviour in adolescents

becomes particularly significant, especially in situations characterised by high impulsivity

and difficulty coping with stressors [29,30].

       Between 1990 and 2017, suicide rates among Ecuadorian adolescents were high, with

approximately 13,024 deaths during this period, translating into 135,731 years of life lost due

to premature mortality [25,26]. Each year, more than a thousand Ecuadorians take their own

lives. In 2019, a very high-risk indicator was observed (n women = 322%; n men = 480%). A

nationwide longitudinal study found that Ecuador had an average suicide rate of 8.8 per

100,000 inhabitants by 2020, placing it among the ten countries with the highest suicide rates

in the region [26,30].

       Self-inflicted injuries were the third leading cause of death among Ecuadorians aged 5

to 17 years in 2022 (n = 120; 7.7%) and in the group aged 18 to 29 years (n = 413; 6.8%). In

the first group, these injuries were surpassed by land transport accidents (n = 195; 12.5%) and

homicides (n = 162; 10.4%), while in the second group, homicides (n = 1933; 32%) and land

transport accidents (n = 1308; 21.6%) were more prevalent [29].

       In Ecuador, records of suicidal behaviour remain inadequate. From a psychiatric

perspective, this phenomenon is often overlooked, as it is not classified as a distinct entity in

the DSM-5, which only recognises associated symptoms. Furthermore, the National Institute

of Census and Statistics (INEC) from Ecuador uses the ICD-10 system, which identifies

suicide and self-harm. However, in cases of completed suicide, only the method used is

typically recorded, without further context or contributing factors [31].
        This study aimed to evaluate the rates of suicidal behaviour in adolescents

experiencing emotional crises and to examine their relationship with psychiatric diagnoses

and demographic factors in the Ecuadorian population.
                                             Methods

Participants

       The medical records database from hospitalised patients in the Critical Care Unit

(CIU) of the Neuroscience Institute of Guayaquil (INC) between 2011 and 2023 was

reviewed. A total of 1026 records were identified, of which 341 cases involved adolescents

aged 12 to 18. Hospitalised patients experiencing an emotional crisis involving depression, in

whom suicidal ideation or a suicide attempt were present, were included (n = 252). Eighty-

nine cases were excluded due to incomplete data in the registries.

Instruments

       On the time of admission, all patients were required to complete an ad hoc

demographic questionnaire. The questionnaire collected data on sex (male or female), age,

marital status (single, married, or divorced), and educational level (elementary, secondary,

high school, or university). It also recorded prior hospitalisations at the INC and the history

of previous suicidal behaviour (suicidal ideation or suicide attempt), along with the method

used in any attempt. Additionally, participants provided information on their lifetime use of

alcohol or drugs, as well as the areas of life they found most concerning (e.g., school, family,

legal issues, relationships, or personal matters).

Procedure

       Retrospective data were obtained by analysing the medical records of adolescent

patients admitted to the ICU during the study period. The review of clinical records was

conducted between 30 August and 5 December 2023. The study protocol was approved by

the Human Research Ethics Committee of Luis Vernaza Hospital (CEISH-HLV), with

approval code 08-EO-CEISH-HLV-2023. This research adhered to the International Ethical

Guidelines for Health-Related Research Involving Humans prepared by the Council of

International Organizations of Medical Sciences (CIOMS).
 Statistical Analysis

       Pearson's Chi-square test (χ²) was used to assess differences between male and female

participants on the method of suicide, alcohol/drug abuse, suicidal behaviour and areas of

concern. Logistic regression analyses examined the relationship between psychiatric

diagnoses (based on ICD-10 classification) and the likelihood of suicidal behaviour. This

method was suitable as it modelled a dichotomous dependent variable (suicidal ideation or

attempt) based on several independent variables (psychiatric diagnoses and age).

       Regression coefficients were reported for each predictor, along with their standard

errors (SE), Z statistic values, and corresponding p values, to determine the statistical

significance of each predictor. The coefficients were interpreted to estimate the effect of each

diagnosis on the likelihood of suicidal ideation or attempt.

       To assess the model fit and calculate its predictive capacity, Cox and Snell’s R² was

computed. Statistical analyses were performed using Jamovi and R Studio software.
                                             Results

       Participants’ diagnoses showed that depressive episodes were the most frequent (n =

185; 73%), followed by bipolar affective disorder (n = 16; 6.3%) and recurrent depressive

disorder (n = 24; 9.5%). Other diagnoses included eating disorders (n = 6; 2.4%), dysthymia

(n = 1; 0.4%), other mood disorders (n = 13; 5.2%), post-traumatic stress disorder (n = 4;

1.6%), mania with psychotic symptoms (n = 1; 0.4%), obsessive-compulsive disorder (n = 1;

0.4%), and mixed anxiety and depression disorder (n = 1; 0.4%). Only 22 (8.7%) patients

reported substance use, including alcohol (n = 1; 0.4%), and other drugs (n = 21; 8.3%).

       The methods of suicide varied among participants. Self-inflicted injury was the most

common method (n = 81; 32%), followed by ingestion of psychotropic substances (n = 40;

16%), poisoning (n = 9; 3.6%), jumping from a height (n = 6; 2.4%), hanging (n = 5; 2.0%),

and the use of firearms (n = 1; 0.4%). Suicidal behaviour was predominantly characterised by

a combination of suicidal ideation and attempts (n = 143; 64%), while fewer participants

reported only suicidal ideation (n = 79; 35%) or a suicide attempt without ideation (n = 3;

1.3%). For more details, see Table 1.

title: Low Flexibility of Emotion Regulation in Adults with Childhood Maltreatment Experiences
abstract:
Background: Childhood maltreatment is associated with a higher risk for developing psychological disorders, such as depression or anxiety. Dysfunctional emotion regulation (ER) has been implicated as a neurocognitive mechanism linking adverse childhood experiences to an increased risk for psychopathological outcomes. 
Objective: This preregistered study investigated the relation between childhood maltreatment and different aspects of ER, including implementation capacity and effort as well as tendency and flexibility in strategy selection.
Methods: In a non-clinical adult sample (N = 227), childhood maltreatment was assessed with the Childhood Trauma Questionnaire. In two experimental ER tasks, participants viewed pictures of varying emotional intensity while applying one of three ER strategies (distraction, reappraisal, suppression) or without any active regulation. The ER implementation task allowed to assess emotion reactivity, implementation capacity, and subjective effort for ER. The ER selection task allowed to assess tendency and flexibility in strategy selection. 
Results: Childhood maltreatment was associated with a lower capacity to implement reappraisal (r = -.12), a stronger tendency to choose suppression (r = .15), and lower flexibility in ER strategy selection (r = -.12). Contrary to our hypotheses, there was no significant association to experimentally assessed emotion reactivity or subjective effort exerted on ER.
Conclusions: We show that people with childhood maltreatment experiences do not only choose suppression more frequently but seem to have a deficit in flexibly adjusting strategy selection to context factors, e.g., stimulus intensity. This can be interpreted as an important factor rendering emotion regulation less adaptive in a clinical sense.

d.armbrustergenc@rptu.de



Keywords: Childhood Maltreatment, Adverse Childhood Experiences, Emotion Regulation

Flexibility, Suppression
 Highlights



•   Childhood maltreatment as well as a range of psychological disorders have been shown to

    be associated with emotion regulation difficulties.

•   Here, we replicate the association between childhood maltreatment and a more frequent

    use of suppression in an experimental design.

•   Childhood maltreatment was associated with a lower capacity for reappraisal and less

    flexibility in emotion regulation.

•   Emotion regulation effort was not associated with childhood maltreatment.

•   These findings shed light on the cognitive mechanisms underlying emotion regulation

    difficulties after childhood maltreatment.




                                                                                            2
 Abstract



Background: Childhood maltreatment is associated with a higher risk for developing

psychological disorders, such as depression or anxiety. Dysfunctional emotion regulation (ER)

has been implicated as a neurocognitive mechanism linking adverse childhood experiences to

an increased risk for psychopathological outcomes.

Objective: This preregistered study investigated the relation between childhood maltreatment

and different aspects of ER, including implementation capacity and effort as well as tendency

and flexibility in strategy selection.

Methods: In a non-clinical adult sample (N = 227), childhood maltreatment was assessed with

the Childhood Trauma Questionnaire. In two experimental ER tasks, participants viewed

pictures of varying emotional intensity while applying one of three ER strategies (distraction,

reappraisal, suppression) or without any active regulation. The ER implementation task allowed

to assess emotion reactivity, implementation capacity, and subjective effort for ER. The ER

selection task allowed to assess tendency and flexibility in strategy selection.

Results: Childhood maltreatment was associated with a lower capacity to implement

reappraisal (r = -.12), a stronger tendency to choose suppression (r = .15), and lower flexibility

in ER strategy selection (r = -.12). Contrary to our hypotheses, there was no significant

association to experimentally assessed emotion reactivity or subjective effort exerted on ER.

Conclusions: We show that people with childhood maltreatment experiences do not only

choose suppression more frequently but seem to have a deficit in flexibly adjusting strategy

selection to context factors, e.g., stimulus intensity. This can be interpreted as an important

factor rendering emotion regulation less adaptive in a clinical sense.




                                                                                                3
 Introduction


Childhood maltreatment, such as the experience of physical or emotional abuse and neglect, is

associated with a higher risk for developing psychiatric disorders, for example depression or

anxiety disorders (Gilbert et al., 2009; Kessler et al., 2010). There have been increasing research

efforts to understand the mechanisms by which this risk is being transferred (e.g., Hoppen &

Chalder, 2018; McCrory et al., 2012). Dysfunctional emotion regulation (ER) has been

implicated as a neurocognitive mechanism linking adverse childhood experiences and an

increased risk for psychopathological outcomes since alterations in ER have been shown in

both psychopathology (Aldao et al., 2010) as well as after childhood maltreatment (e.g.,

Weissman et al., 2019).



Emotion Regulation

Emotion regulation refers to the processes through which the quality, extent, and / or duration

of an emotion can be changed (J. J. Gross & Thompson, 2007). Emotion reactivity can be

conceptualised as a component of emotion regulation (Becerra & Campitelli, 2013) and is

defined as the individual activation by emotions, their intensity, and duration (Becerra &

Campitelli, 2013; Davidson, 1998). Different stages of ER have been proposed, including the

identification of the need to regulate, the selection of a specific emotion regulation strategy, its

implementation, and the monitoring of the ER success (J. J. Gross, 2015). There is a multitude

of ER strategies which have been conceptualised along the time course of an emotional

response within the framework of the process model of ER (J. J. Gross, 1998b). Distraction as

one important disengagement strategy is the reorientation of attention away from the emotional

stimulus is an early way to regulate an emotional response, i.e., before it even starts. This is

opposed to the engagement strategy reappraisal as cognitively changing the meaning of an



                                                                                                  4
 emotional event at a later point. Towards the end of emotion generation, expressive suppression

is the not showing of any emotional response in mimics, gestures, or body posture.

While the early ER literature (e.g., J. J. Gross, 1998a) categorized strategies into adaptive ones

(e.g., reappraisal) vs. maladaptive ones (e.g., suppression), more recent research suggests that

factors such as the context in which ER is taking place have to be taken into account (Aldao et

al., 2015). For example, the controllability of the situation has been shown to exert differential

effects: under circumstances characterised by diminished individual control, reappraisal is

associated with lower depressiveness as opposed to when the individual actually could change

the situation – under these circumstances reappraisal has been shown to be associated with

higher levels of depressiveness (Troy et al., 2013). Thus, recent accounts point out the

importance of flexibility in emotion regulation (Bonanno & Burton, 2013; Kobylińska & Kusev,

2019).

There is a great conceptual heterogeneity in the definition of ER flexibility (e.g., Aldao et al.,

2015; Bonanno & Burton, 2013). Here, we focus on a theoretical framework based on the

process model of ER (J. J. Gross, 1998b) proposed by Sheppes et al. (2014). This framework

suggests that the regulation of more intense emotions requires more cognitive resources,

especially with engagement strategies like reappraisal. To maintain a balance between costs,

i.e., the use of cognitive resources, and the benefit of regulating emotions, it would be most

beneficial to disengage from high emotional intensity stimuli early on, e.g., with a

disengagement strategy like distraction. However, this would come with motivational long-

term costs of reduced processing and remembering of emotional stimuli and the potential of

compromising adaptive learning to achieve one’s goals (Sheppes, 2020). In contrast,

engagement strategies allow for deeper processing of a stimulus and intervene at a later point,

e.g., reappraisal. Consequently, ER flexibility can be defined as selecting distraction as ER

strategy for emotional stimuli of high intensity, while selecting reappraisal for low intensity

stimuli. In a series of studies, Sheppes et al. (2014) showed that healthy individuals do actually

                                                                                                5
 prefer to regulate emotions elicited by high intensity stimuli by distraction, whereas reappraisal

was preferred for emotions elicited by low intensity stimuli which is assumed to be the adaptive

pattern associated with more positive consequences for mental health (for a review see Sheppes,

2020).



Emotion Regulation in Psychopathology

Previous research shows that an inflexible tendency of choosing the emotion regulation

strategies suppression, rumination, and avoidance characterizes different kinds of

psychopathology (Lincoln et al., 2022). It has also been suggested that individual differences

in emotion reactivity might partly underly emotional problems in psychopathology (J. J. Gross,

2015). Emotion regulation difficulties are, for example, one key symptom in Borderline

personality disorder (e.g., Daros & Williams, 2019; Kuo & Linehan, 2009). Anxiety disorders

are characterized by debilitating feelings of fear or worry and have been associated with a

habitual use of rumination (Olatunji et al., 2013). Individuals with major depression prefer

strategies such as suppression (Beblo et al., 2012) and rumination (Olatunji et al., 2013). Also

in non-clinical samples, a more frequent use of suppression, rumination, and distraction is

associated with higher levels of psychopathological symptoms (Cutuli, 2014; Moulds et al.,

2007; Rammensee et al., 2023). Further evidence on emotion dysregulation in psychopathology

stems from studies on PTSD (Ehring & Quack, 2010) and substance use disorders (Weiss et al.,

2022) – often employing self-report measures such as the widely used Difficulties in Emotion

Regulation Scale (DERS). In light of its relative recent introduction and conceptual

heterogeneity, research on emotion regulation flexibility is a growing field of research (e.g.,

Conroy et al., 2020; Goubet & Chrysikou, 2019; Grommisch et al., 2020). For example, in a

recent study by Chen and Bonanno (2021) employing self-report measures, it has been shown

that individuals with an inflexible profile of emotion regulation, defined as showing low context



                                                                                                6
 sensitivity, a small repertoire of available ER strategies, and low sensitivity to feedback, exhibit

higher levels of depressive and anxiety symptoms.

Given these findings, emotion regulation is an important target of psychotherapeutic

intervention (Berking et al., 2008) and it has been proposed as a transdiagnostic mechanism of

change in psychotherapy (Gratz et al., 2015). For example, an improvement in emotion

regulation strategy application over the course of cognitive behavioural therapy has been shown

to be associated with symptom reduction in major depression (Radkovsky et al., 2014).



Emotion Regulation after Childhood Maltreatment

Given the tremendous developmental changes of the psychological and neural bases of ER

during childhood and adolescence (Silvers, 2022), it is plausible to assume that adverse

experiences during childhood do have an impact on ER-related processes. For example, the

tripartite model of familial influence on the development of ER posits that children are shaped

by observing their parent’s ER, by the parenting practices related to emotion processing, and

the family’s general emotional climate (Morris et al., 2007). Previous research – again, mostly

based on self-report measures – has shown that children, adolescents, and adults with

experiences of childhood maltreatment report higher emotion reactivity (Lavi et al., 2019;

Weissman et al., 2019) and a more frequent use of suppression and rumination, which is

associated with higher psychopathological symptoms (Balan et al., 2017; Rudenstine et al.,

2019; Waizman et al., 2021; Weissman et al., 2019). As young adults, those with a history of

maltreatment are also more likely to show a pattern of ER difficulties (as assessed by the DERS

and other self-report measures of habitual ER), alongside increased negative affect, low

emotional awareness, and less frequent use of reappraisal (Warmingham et al., 2023). A recent

meta-analysis showed a mediation of the relation between childhood adversity and

psychopathology by self-reported difficulties in ER as well as the habitual use of specific



                                                                                                  7
 emotion regulation strategies (i.e., more suppression and rumination, less reappraisal) - but not

by other strategies (i.e., distraction; Miu et al., 2022).

In addition to investigations of habitual ER, which are mainly based on retrospective self-report,

recent studies using ecological momentary assessment (EMA) have shown a relation between

childhood maltreatment experiences and a less frequent use of reappraisal as well as lower

overall ER success in daily life. Notably, however, there were no significant differences in the

use of suppression or rumination (Ion et al., 2023). To further understand differences in the use

of different ER strategies associated to childhood maltreatment experience, researchers have

begun to investigate the subjective effort related to the implementation of different strategies

(see, for example, Scheffel et al., 2021 for one possible assessment of effort related to ER). A

neuroimaging study in adolescents with maltreatment experience found no differences in the

actual capacity to regulate emotions using reappraisal, but an increased activation of prefrontal

control regions during regulation (McLaughlin et al., 2015). This was interpreted as increased

use of neural resources and could indicate higher effort exerted during the implementation of

reappraisal. However, using EMA data, Ion et al. (2023) found no associations between self-

reported subjective effort during ER and childhood maltreatment.

Studies on the association between childhood maltreatment and flexibility in emotion

regulation are scarce. In a study on expressive flexibility it was shown that higher levels of

childhood maltreatment were associated with a lower ability to flexibly adjust emotion

expression to situational demands (Pițur & Miu, 2020). Applying the conceptualisation of ER

flexibility as an adaptive selection of ER strategy according to context (Aldao et al., 2015;

Sheppes et al., 2014), a study in firefighters showed a moderation effect of ER flexibility in that

there was a positive correlation between PTSD symptom severity and the number of

experienced traumatic events only in those with low ER flexibility (Levy-Gigi et al., 2016).

This implicates ER flexibility as a protective factor when facing adversity, although it is an

open question how early adversity might affect the development of ER flexibility.

                                                                                                 8
 To summarize, there is a large body of research showing altered emotion regulation after

childhood maltreatment which is mainly based on self-report questionnaire measures assessing

habitual use of different emotion regulation strategies or general difficulties in emotion

regulation. This research shows an association between childhood maltreatment and a higher

tendency to use suppre

title: Psychological Inflexibility in Written Language: Convergent Validity and Associations with Posttraumatic Stress Symptomology and Impairment
abstract: Expressive writing, particularly written emotional disclosure (WED), has a range of beneficial outcomes, including reductions in posttraumatic stress (PTS) symptomology, and interventions that employ WED are effective in the treatment of PTSD. Theory and empirical evidence supports the role of psychological inflexibility in the development and maintenance of PTS symptoms, and recent studies have begun to analyze psychological inflexibility in verbal contexts, including WED exercises. The content of WED narratives is a rich source of information in examining underlying theories of PTS symptomology, however this area is virtually unexplored with regard to psychological inflexibility. The present study sought to examine the validity of psychological inflexibility coded in WED narratives in a community sample (N = 415). Results support the convergent validity of coding written psychological inflexibility, as seen by negative associations with self-report psychological flexibility and positive associations with PTS symptoms and anxiety-related distress and impairment. Further, written psychological inflexibility predicts PTS-related outcomes over and above variance explained by self-report psychological flexibility, history of cumulative exposure to potentially traumatic events, and narrative word counts. Findings from the present study extend the small but growing literature on the validity and utility of evaluating features of psychological inflexibility in language.

title: ‘Equal-unblinding’ meta-analysis of psychedelic therapy vs. antidepressants for the treatment of depression
abstract:
Importance: Psychedelic-assisted therapy (PAT) trials have high levels of functional unblinding. This effect positively biases results when PAT trials are compared against truly blinded trials. 

Objective:  This pre-registered meta-analysis investigated the comparative efficacy of PAT and open-label traditional antidepressants (tAD; such as SSRIs and SNRIs) for the treatment of major depression. The rationale is that PAT is effectively always open-label, thus, it is only fair to compare results against open-label tAD trials, so both interventions equally benefit from effects associated with patients knowing the treatment.

Data Sources:  PubMed was systematically searched for trials of PAT and open-label tAD for the treatment of major depression without comorbidity in outpatient, non-psychotic adults. 24 of the initially retrieved 619 records met inclusion.

Data Extraction and Synthesis: Depression scores were extracted by two independent reviewers; estimates were pooled with both Bayesian and frequentist mixed-effects models. The reporting follows the PRISMA guideline. 

Main Outcome(s) and Measure(s): Following pre-defined hypothesis, we compared the mean within-arm effect size from baseline to primary endpoint, i.e. the patient improvement, between PAT and open-label tAD trials on the 17-item Hamilton Depression Rating Scale. We also compared the within-arm effect size of blinded vs. open-label trials in both PAT and tAD, to assess the influence of blinding. 

Results: In total, 8 PAT trials involving 548 patients and 16 open-label tAD studies involving 9751 patients were included. Contrary to prior hypothesis, PAT was no more effective than open-label tAD treatment (estimated difference: 0.3 favoring open-label tAD; 95% confidence interval: [-1.39, 1.98]; p=0.730). Open-label tAD was associated with better outcomes than blinded treatment (1.3 [0.07, 2.51]; p=0.038), but the same difference was not observed in PAT (0.4 [-2.20, 3.11]; p=0.738).

Conclusions and Relevance: Both tAD and PAT were associated with robust, statistically, and clinically meaningful improvements. However, PAT’s lack of superiority compared to tADs under equal-unblinding conditions highlights the influence of blinding integrity and presents a sobering viewpoint on the treatment’s potential.

title: An open-label, dose-escalation trial of  psilocybin-assisted therapy for bipolar 2  depression
abstract:
Background: Individuals with bipolar II disorder (BD-II) and depression face limited treatment 
options and are often excluded from psilocybin therapy trials due to theoretical concerns of 
precipitating mania or psychosis.  Although psilocybin has demonstrated antidepressant effects 
when combined with psychotherapy, adverse event reporting is inconsistent, and restrictive 
eligibility criteria limit generalizability.  
Aims: To evaluate the safety, tolerability, and preliminary efficacy of psilocybin therapy in 
individuals with BD-II experiencing moderate-to-severe depression. 
Method: In this open-label, single-arm pilot trial, 14 participants received 10 mg of psilocybin, 
followed by 25 mg if depressive symptoms persisted. Participants underwent psychotherapy 
before, during, and after psilocybin administration sessions and were proactively monitored for 
adverse events. Depression and quality of life were assessed using the Montgomery-Asberg 
Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) and Quality of Life in Bipolar Disorder Questionnaire (QoL
BD), along with exploratory measures. 
Results: Psilocybin was well tolerated, with transient increases in heart rate and blood pressure 
and no serious adverse events. Common adverse events included mild-to-moderate anxiety, 
nausea, and headache. Three participants experienced notable psychiatric adverse events 
(suicidal ideation and hypomania) which resolved with support. The frequency and nature of 
both serious and non-serious adverse events were broadly comparable to those reported in 
psilocybin studies for other conditions. MADRS scores improved at all timepoints: 21 days after 
10 mg (-12.7 [2.7], p<0.001); 21 days after 25 mg (-18.6 [3.1], p<0.001) and 90 days after the 
final dose (-14.3 [2.8], p<0.001). Quality of life also improved at 90 days (31.2 [10.2], p=0.004).  
Conclusions: Psilocybin therapy under controlled conditions may be safe and effective in 
reducing depressive symptoms and improving quality of life in individuals with BD-II depression, 
and feared outcomes—mania, psychosis, and suicidality—do not appear elevated relative to 
other clinical populations treated with psilocybin. Given the high psychiatric comorbidity and 
general vulnerability to adverse events in this population, randomized, placebo-controlled trials 
are needed to confirm efficacy and refine dosing protocols in larger samples.

title: Self-harm in autistic and non-autistic people: network structure and prediction of suicidality over time
abstract:
Background
Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) is relatively common among autistic people. While studies indicate relationships between NSSI and suicidal thoughts and behaviour (STB), it is unclear whether specific NSSI behaviours co-occur in meaningful ways with differential relevance to STB, and if this differs between autistic and non-autistic people.
Methods
To understand the clinical relevance of specific NSSI behaviours to STB and related constructs, we pooled data from 327 autistic and 260 non-autistic participants from two online surveys. We used network analyses to separately visualise and compare the NSSI networks of autistic and non-autistic people, controlling for age differences between groups. Thereafter, using data provided by autistic people across two time-points one year apart, we examined zero-order correlations and used LASSO regression to assess links between NSSI behaviours at the first time-point and STB-related variables at the second.
Results
Our first analyses revealed differences between autistic and non-autistic people in the co-occurrence of NSSI behaviours and their relationships to STB-related constructs. Three NSSI clusters emerged for autistic people and one for non-autistic people. In both groups, some NSSI behaviours were only indirectly linked to STB via relationships to other NSSI behaviours; cutting and ingesting dangerous substances/sharp objects were most strongly related to suicide attempts. Our second analyses, focusing on autistic people, revealed initial correlations between cutting, self-burning, self-hitting and skin-tearing and STB-related outcomes, although these disappeared after controlling for STB at time-point one. Self-biting, breaking bones and fighting to get hurt remained significant predictors of STB-related outcomes. 
Conclusion
While requiring replication in better matched samples, our findings corroborate differences in how autistic and non-autistic people engage in NSSI, as well as differential relevance of specific behaviours to STB. Future research must explore direct and indirect pathways between NSSI behaviours and STB, and the functional needs that different behaviours serve.

Running title: NSSI networks in autistic people

Keywords (4-6): Self-harm; suicide; network analysis




                                                                                     2
 Abstract

Background

Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) is relatively common among autistic people. While studies

indicate relationships between NSSI and suicidal thoughts and behaviour (STB), it is unclear

whether specific NSSI behaviours co-occur in meaningful ways with differential relevance to

STB, and if this differs between autistic and non-autistic people.

Methods

To understand the clinical relevance of specific NSSI behaviours to STB and related

constructs, we pooled data from 327 autistic and 260 non-autistic participants from two

online surveys. We used network analyses to separately visualise and compare the NSSI

networks of autistic and non-autistic people, controlling for age differences between groups.

Thereafter, using data provided by autistic people across two time-points one year apart, we

examined zero-order correlations and used LASSO regression to assess links between NSSI

behaviours at the first time-point and STB-related variables at the second.

Results

       Our first analyses revealed differences between autistic and non-autistic people in the

co-occurrence of NSSI behaviours and their relationships to STB-related constructs. Three

NSSI clusters emerged for autistic people and one for non-autistic people. In both groups,

some NSSI behaviours were only indirectly linked to STB via relationships to other NSSI

behaviours; cutting and ingesting dangerous substances/sharp objects were most strongly

related to suicide attempts. Our second analyses, focusing on autistic people, revealed initial

correlations between cutting, self-burning, self-hitting and skin-tearing and STB-related

outcomes, although these disappeared after controlling for STB at time-point one. Self-biting,

breaking bones and fighting to get hurt remained significant predictors of STB-related

outcomes.


                                                                                                  3
 Conclusion

       While requiring replication in better matched samples, our findings corroborate

differences in how autistic and non-autistic people engage in NSSI, as well as differential

relevance of specific behaviours to STB. Future research must explore direct and indirect

pathways between NSSI behaviours and STB, and the functional needs that different

behaviours serve.




                                                                                              4
 Community brief



Why is this an important issue?

Self-harm affects many autistic people and may contribute to high suicide rates in the autistic

community. Autistic people hurt themselves in different ways. However, we do not currently

know if some self-harming behaviours are more strongly related to suicide than others.



What was the purpose of this study?

We aimed to explore which self-harming behaviours tend to occur together in autistic and

non-autistic people, and how these relate to suicidal thoughts and behaviour, depression, and

mental states associated with suicidality.



What did the researchers do?

We analysed data from two online surveys completed by autistic and non-autistic people.

First, we used network analysis, which visualises the relationships between behaviours,

thoughts and emotions, to plot self-harm networks for autistic and non-autistic people.

Secondly, using a subset of autistic participants who had provided data across two time-

points, one year apart, we examined if self-harming behaviours at the first time-point could

predict suicidal thoughts and experiences at the second.



What were the results and conclusions of the study?

Our first analyses showed that self-harming behaviours co-occur in different patterns between

autistic and non-autistic people, and they relate differently to suicidal experiences. In autistic

people, some self-harming behaviours, like self-biting, were only very weakly or indirectly

linked to suicidality. Other behaviours, like cutting, were more closely linked to suicide


                                                                                                     5
 attempts. Our second analyses suggested that certain behaviours, like cutting, self-biting and

fighting to get hurt, predicted suicidal experiences one year later. They also showed that

suicidal thoughts and mental states were stable across both time-points.



What is new or controversial about these findings?

While previous studies have suggested a relationship between self-harm and suicide in

autistic people, we demonstrated that specific self-harm behaviours differ in their

relationships with suicidality. This possibility was suggested by previous qualitative research

which found that autistic people differentiated between self-harming behaviours associated

with sensory stimulation, those associated with meltdowns, and those linked to life stress,

mental health conditions and suicidality. Our findings also suggest that autistic people and

non-autistic people engage in self-harm in slightly different ways, and so might need different

support.



What are potential weaknesses in the study?

Our autistic sample did not include autistic people with higher support needs. Not all of the

measurement tools we used were validated in autistic people. Even though certain self-

harming behaviours predicted suicidal experiences one-year later, this does not mean that

self-harming behaviours caused or made these suicidal experiences more likely to happen.

Autistic participants were significantly older than non-autistic participants, which may have

affected the findings.



How will these findings help autistic adults now or in the future?

As self-harm is relatively common in autistic people, understanding the relevance of specific

behaviours to mental health conditions and suicidality may help us identify people at risk and


                                                                                                  6
 those who need support. All forms of self-harm should be taken seriously in autistic people,

but better understanding about different forms of self-harm may help us identify what kind of

support is needed.




                                                                                               7
         Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) is a significant correlate of suicidal thoughts and

behaviour (STB) in autistic people.1-3 Previous studies have found that NSSI is relatively

common4-6 and often serves functions other than indicating suicidal intent.7,8 It is presently

unclear whether there is clinical significance in the form and heterogeneity of NSSI

behaviours among autistic people. To address this, we examined cross-sectional relationships

between NSSI behaviours and indices of STB, and whether certain behaviours are associated

with suicidality over time.

        Self-injury occurs with a lifetime prevalence of 42% in autistic people,9 but this

umbrella term is inclusive of behaviours with varied features and purposes, including self-

injury in the course of suicide attempts. The prevalence of self-injury which is non-suicidal,

reportedly or seemingly not enacted as part of a suicide attempt, is unknown. Within this

category, “self-injurious behaviour” (SIB) is often used to describe self-injury which occurs

among autistic (and non-autistic10) people with more significant intellectual difficulties, often

without clear intent to cause pain or injury, and not ostensibly as part of a suicide attempt.11

Often conceptualised as a manifestation of stereotypy, aggression or ‘challenging

behaviour’,12,13 SIB commonly presents as repetitive behaviours like head banging, self-

biting and hair-pulling,9 which may occur in the presence of other people, without apparent

delayed gratification, and without use of implements. SIB is often differentiated from self-

injurious behaviour which is episodic yet purposeful, planned and/or hidden, often involves

complex behaviours involving implements, and is, according to personal accounts , enacted

with intent to cause non-lethal injury and/or pain.13 This second manifestation of non-suicidal

self-injury, occurring in autistic people who can self-report their experiences, seems

behaviourally and functionally akin to NSSI as a transdiagnostic phenomenon linked with

psychopathology and STB14-16; accordingly, we,7 and others,2,17 describe it using the same

term.


                                                                                                   8
         Autistic people are more likely than non-autistic people to engage in NSSI throughout

their lifespans,4-6,18 but appear to do so in similar ways, for similar reasons, and with

apparently similar repercussions.7,8,17,19 Although NSSI behaviours vary, the most common

forms in both autistic and non-autistic groups are self-scratching and/or pinching, cutting or

carving the skin, and hitting oneself or other objects.3,6,15,17 A body of research suggests that,

as in non-autistic people,20-22 NSSI in autistic people principally operates as a means of

intrapersonal regulation (i.e. of emotional states, sensory and cognitive overload). However,

it can also serve as a means of communication, generating sensation, self-punishment and,

conversely, avoiding lethal suicide attempts7,8,17,19,23,24; and there may be additional, unique

functional purposes of NSSI in autistic people.8,25 Beyond the functionality of NSSI, there is

evidence for a directional association between NSSI and STB in non-autistic people,14,26-28

which may partially operate via deleterious impacts of NSSI on psychosocial functioning and

relationships,29 emotional dysregulation,30 and psychopathology.31,32 While some autistic

people express neutrality or positive feelings about NSSI,7 others corroborate negative

impacts of NSSI on relationships, self-care, mental health and self-worth.7,19,33 Moreover, a

cross-sectional evidence base is emerging to support elevated suicide risk in autistic people

with a history of NSSI.1-3,34,35

        Beyond this broad association between NSSI and STB, research from non-autistic

populations suggests there may be clinical and predictive utility in the particular forms and

features of NSSI. This research links heightened suicide risk to more frequent engagement in

NSSI, greater severity of injury, instances of unintentional severity, combining behaviours,

and, in particular, engagement in a wider range of NSSI behaviours.36-39 There may also be

meaningful distinctions between individual NSSI behaviours in relation to factors such as the

pain, severity, and visibility of the damage they cause. Cutting and skin-carving yield

immediate, lasting and potentially graphic tissue damage; they approximate means of


                                                                                                     9
 attempting suicide, and are theorised to gradually desensitise people to pain and fear of dying

(i.e. such that they acquire ‘capability’ for suicide).40,41 Accordingly, several studies have

highlighted that these behaviours are particularly strongly linked with psychopathology and

STB.26,42-45 Self-hitting, a behaviour which incurs less obvious or immediate tissue damage,

is sometimes46 but not always43,45 associated with STB. Rarer behaviours, such as self-

burning and ingestion of dangerous substances, have also been highlighted in association

with suicidal ideation and attempts in non-autistic people.47

       Presently we do not know whether, among autistic people, different NSSI behaviours

have differential relevance to STB and other clinical outcomes. Interestingly, qualitative work

suggests that, much as academics differentiate between SIB and NSSI,17 autistic people

distinguish between two different manifestations of self-injury.8,19,48 The first occurs

unpremeditated, instinctively, and sometimes publicly in the context of meltdowns or self-

stimulation (“autistic self-harm”,8 a “meltdown kind of thing”19, over which the person has

“no control”48); it often involves behaviours such as self-hitting, slapping or head-

banging8,19,48. The second occurs in a controlled, premeditated and private manner

(“considered self-harm”,19 or “conventional” or “non-autism-related” self-harm8, “intentional

self-harm… the kind of self-harm you have control over and I choose to do it”48). Autistic
